Brian wanted to know what violence meant] That night he had a stomach ache. Topics covered: Static Equilibrium, Stability, Rope Walker. Static equilibrium is only achieved when the net external force AND net external torque on an object are both zero. Instructor/speaker: Prof. Walter Lewin Date recorded: November 10, 1999 Please make sure you play the Video before clicking the links below. Rotation vs. Translation Stability of a Ladder Rope Tension Reduced by Friction Locating the Center of Mass of a Rigid Body Stability of a Rope Walker During the past four lectures, we have dealt with angular momentum, torques and rolling objects and rotations. And many of you then think, "Oh, my goodness, now we have to remember a whole zoo of equations," but that's not true. If you simply know how to make a conversion from linear symbols to rotation, which is immediately trivial, of course-- position becomes angle; velocity becomes angular velocity; acceleration becomes angular acceleration, and so on-- then you can make the conversions very easily. If you remember that the kinetic energy is one-half m v squared, then the kinetic energy of rotation then becomes one-half I omega squared. This is on the Web. Every view graph that I show you in lectures is always on the Web, and you should look under "lecture supplements," and then you can make yourself a hard copy. So I thought this might be useful for you to remember. Today I want to discuss in detail what it takes for an object to be in complete static equilibrium. For an object to be in static equilibrium, it is not enough that the sum of all forces is zero. But what is also required, that the sum of all torques relative to any point that you choose is also zero. And that will be the topic today. If this is an object free in space and let's say the center of mass is here, and I have a force on this object in this direction and another force on this object in opposite direction but equal in magnitude, then the sum of all forces is zero. But you better believe it that there is no equilibrium. There is a torque... and if this distance equals b, then the torque on that object relative to any point that you choose-- it doesn't matter which one you take-- the magnitude of that will be b times f. And if there is a torque, there's going to be an angular acceleration. Torque is I alpha. And so it's going to rotate. In this case, it will rotate about the center of mass, so it's not static equilibrium. The torque in this case would be out of the blackboard. So it's going to rotate like this. So we got to keep a close eye on torques, as much as we have to on the forces themselves. So, today I have chosen a ladder as my subject of static equilibrium. I put a ladder against a wall. Here's the wall, and this is the ladder. At point P, where the wall is, there is almost... let's say there is no friction. Mu of P is zero. At point Q here, where it's resting on the floor, there is friction. The static friction at point Q-- we'll simply call that mu. The ladder has a mass M, and it has a length l. So here is the center of mass of that ladder, right in the middle. And this angle equals alpha. We know from experience that if this angle is too small that the ladder will slide, and so I want to make the topic today "What should that angle be so that it does not slide?" Well, we have here a force Mg; that's gravity. Then we have a normal force here-- I call that NQ. We have friction in this direction, because clearly the ladder wants to slide like this, so the frictional force will try to prevent that, will be in this direction. At point P there is no friction, so there can only be a normal force. And I call that N of P. So these are the only forces that act on this object. And now we can start our exercise. We can say, all right, the sum of all forces in the x direction have to be zero. So that means that N of P must be in magnitude the same as the frictional force. Now the sum of all forces in the y direction have to be zero. So that means that Mg must be N of Q. N of Q must be Mg. But now we need that the sum of all torques have to be zero. It doesn't matter which point you choose-- you can pick any point. You can pick a point here, or here, or there, or there. You can pick C, you can pick P. I choose Q. The reason why I choose Q is because then I lose both this force and that force, and I only have to deal with this one and that one. And so I'm going to take the torque relative to point Q. So we have NP. We have the cross-product of the position vector to this point times the force. Since the length is l and the angle is alpha, this is l sine alpha, so I'm going to get NP times l sine alpha. And I call the torque that is in the blackboard, I call that my positive direction, and the torque that is out of the blackboard, I give that a negative direction. So this one is in the blackboard, so I call that positive. The next one, Mg, is going to be negative torque, and so now I need the cross-product between the position vector and Mg, so that is the length here, which is one-half l cosine alpha times Mg-- Mg one-half l cosine alpha, and that now must be zero. So I find, then, that N of P-- that is, the normal force at point P-- I lose my l, equals M divided by two times the cotangent of alpha, and that must be the frictional force. So I know what the frictional force is given by this result. Now, I don't want this ladder to slide, so now I have a requirement that the frictional force must be less or equal to the maximum frictional force possible. And the maximum frictional force at this point Q is mu times the normal force. So my requirement now is that N over 2 times the cotangent of alpha must be less or equal to Mg times mu. Mu times Mg. Did I lose a g here? Yes, I did. I have here a g, and I have here a g. Correct? Because if I have this force here and this position vector, then I have Mg. So there is a g here. And so there is a g here, and so I lose my Mg, and so you'll find that the cotangent of alpha is smaller or equal than 2 mu, or the tangent of alpha is larger or equal than 1 over 2 mu. And so that is the condition for the ladder to be stable. And when you look at this result, it tells you that the larger mu is... the larger mu is, the smaller that angle, and that's very pleasing. That's exactly what you expect. And if mu is very low, then the situation is very unstable. Then it will slide almost at any angle. If we take some numerical examples-- for instance, I take mu equals 0.5, then alpha would be 45 degrees, and if the angle is any less, then it will slide. If you take mu is 0.25, then the critical angle where it will start to slide, I think, is somewhere near 63 degrees, but you can check that for yourself. So the angle has to be larger than that number for the ladder to be stable. This result is very intuitive, namely that if the angle is too small that the ladder starts to slide. I have a ladder here against this wall. We have tried to make this here as smooth as we possibly can. It's not perfect. So it's only a poor-man's version of what I discuss there, but in any case, the friction coefficient with the floor is substantially larger than the friction coefficient here. And what is no surprise to you, that if the angle alpha is too small, then there's no equilibrium. The angle of alpha has to be larger than a certain value, as you see there, and then it's stable. That's all I want you to see now. But now we're going to make the situation more interesting, and in a way I'd like to test your intuition. Suppose I set the angle of the ladder exactly at the critical point. In other words, I'm going to set it so that the cotangent of alpha is exactly 2 mu, so it is hanging in there on its thumbs, just about to start sliding. And now I'm going to ask one of you to walk up that ladder, to start here and slowly walk up that ladder. Do you think that stepping on that ladder, starting off, is super dangerous? That the ladder immediately will start to slide? Or do you think that actually your being at the bottom will make it more stable? Who thinks that it will immediately start to slide? A few people. Who thinks that it will not start to slide when you step on the lowest... We'll see what happens later on. Now, this person is going to climb the ladder, and then there comes a time that it passes point C and reaches point P. Will it now be safe to do that, or do you think that now it's going to be very dangerous? What do you think? Who thinks that you shouldn't get too high up on the ladder? Who thinks it makes no difference-- you can go all the way up to the end? There are always a few very courageous people. Okay, so this is what I'm going to analyze with you, and most of you have the right intuition, but we're going to look at this in a quantitative way as I know how to. So we're going to put a person with mass little m on that ladder, and we put the person here. So this force here is little mg, and let's make this distance d. And now we're going to redo all these calculations. We start completely from scratch. The sum of all forces in the x direction has to be zero. N of P must be F of f. Now the sum of all forces in the y direction has to be zero. Now there is a change. So now we have that N of Q must become larger, must be equal to capital M plus little m times g, so the maximum friction, which is mu times NQ, now becomes mu times M plus m times g. So the maximum friction goes up. Now we need that the torque, and I pick my point Q-- relative to point Q-- Well, the first two terms haven't changed, so I have N of P times l sine alpha minus Mg times one-half l times the cosine of alpha. But now we have a third term, namely this position vector, and this force, and so now we're going to get this distance-- which is d cosine alpha-- times this force. So we have here minus mg times d cosine alpha, and that now equals zero. So I'm going to take N of P out of here. And I can take g cosine alpha out. So if I take g cosine alpha out, I have Ml divided by two left over plus little md, and then I have to divide by l sine theta. Not theta, but alpha. And so now I can write for cosine alpha divided by sine alpha, I can write cotangent alpha, and I'll bring the l inside here, so I have g cotangent alpha times M over 2 plus little md divided by l. And that now must be the frictional force, because NP is still the frictional force. Let me make sure that I have this right. Yes, I do. g cotangent alpha, M over 2, little md over l-- that is the frictional force. Notice that the frictional force is going up, because this term is added, and we didn't have that term before. Before we only had this term that you see here. So you first thought may be that the situation has become more dangerous, because if there is more friction, well, the ladder was set exactly at that critical point-- it was hanging on there by its thumbs. So if the friction goes up, you may say, "My goodness, it will probably start to slide." However, what you overlook, then, is that the maximum friction has also gone up, and so we have to evaluate this now in comparison with the maximum friction. And the best way to do this is to think of this first as making d equal zero. So we said the person starts at the bottom of the ladder and we ask this person to gradually climb up. Now, notice when d equals zero that the frictional force is exactly the same... what it was before-- there is no difference. That frictional force has not changed when d equals zero. But what has changed is the maximum friction. The maximum friction has this little m in it, and that's independent of d. There is no d anywhere here. So if the maximum friction goes up, and the friction itself remains the same, clearly the ladder has become more stable, and so you can step on the lowest tread and nothing will happen. On the contrary, the situation will become more stable. As the person starts to move up, the frictional force gradually increases, because this term goes up, but the maximum friction remains the same-- it's independent of d, and so now there comes a time that this force becomes larger than the maximum friction and then the ladder will start to slide, and that, of course, is what we want to find out now. So now the situation is only safe as long as the frictional force is smaller or equal to the maximum value possible. And that's the case when g cotangent alpha... but remember, we set it at the critical angle, so that cotangent alpha is 2 mu. So I can replace this by 2 mu, because that's the way I set up my experiment. I start that way. I don't start with a random angle; I start exactly at the angle so that the ladder is sort of just hanging in there. So cotangent alpha is 2 mu. M divided by 2 plus m times d divided by l, this now has to be less or equal to this value-- mu times M plus m times g. Notice I lose my g. I lose my mu. I have here 2 times M over 2, which is M, and I have one M here, so I lose my capital M, and so I find that 2md over l has to be less or equal to m. I lose my m, and I find that d has to be smaller or equal to l over 2. And that is not unlike most of your intuition, namely as long as the person who steps on the ladder stays on this side, the situation will become more stable. Certainly when the person starts here, the stability has enormously increased. As you gradually approach that point here-- the center of mass, where d is exactly l over 2-- then, of course, the situation becomes again extremely critical here, but when you're over this point, it's no longer critical, and the ladder will start to slide. So in a nutshell, then, we set the ladder at the critical situation. It's about to start sliding. We put a person on here, it becomes more stable. The person walks up slowly, the frictional force will increase, because of this term, the maximum friction will not change-- it has already gone up because the person is on the ladder-- and as the person approaches this point, the situation becomes less and less stable all the time. Right at this point, we are back to where we were, the situation is about as critical as it was before the person steps on it. And then the person proceeds; then the ladder will slip. And I can show that to you-- at least I can make an attempt. I have here that same ladder, and now what I will do is I will set the angle alpha not exactly at the critical point but a little lower, so that when I let it go, we'll all see that it will slide. So it's past the critical angle; the angle is smaller. But now I have four kilograms here. I wasn't going to ask any one of you to walk up this ladder, believe me, and I wasn't going to do it myself either. So you see it is unstable. And now I put the four kilograms on here. And I can let go, and the ladder has become stable. Do I take it off-- there it goes. So the person standing very low made it more stable, exactly consistent with what we just saw. Now I make the angle alpha a little larger than critical, so the ladder is happy. But now the person is going to do something dangerous. He's going to walk and stand here-- and he shouldn't do that, as you see. That's exactly what you have seen there. So the friction plays an essential role for us to get static equilibrium, and that is often the case. There are many examples in our daily lives where friction can be used to our advantage, and a very special case, which I will discuss with you now, is often used by sailors-- by holding, controlling a very massive object, a very large force, controlling it with a very small one. And you do that by making use of friction. You wrap a rope around a pole, or around a rod, and you use the friction between the rope and that rod to your advantage, and it works as follows. Here is this rod-- it doesn't have to be horizontal as this one-- and I hang on this side of the rod, I hang with a string a very massive object. So the tension here, T2, would be Mg-- assuming that it is not accelerating, that it is at rest. I wrap this rope around here several times, and here I hang an object which is substantially less in mass, which is m, little m, and the tension equals mg. If there is no friction at all in this bar, then... and the rope is near massless, then T1 will be the same as T2, so the situation will start to accelerate. However, if we make use of the friction, then we can have a stable situation. We can have static equilibrium, so that this one is not moving and this one is not moving, and then we can have T2 to be way, way larger than T1, by using friction to our advantage. Let's blow up that center portion. Radius R, and let us have this rope here-- I'll give the rope a color. It doesn't have to come off vertically, of course. It could be at any angle. There it is, and so here is my T1 and here is my T2. And we take the situation that the pull on this side is way larger than on that side so that this rope wants to slip in this direction. That's what it would like to do. If you look at these small little sections of the rope, it is immediately obvious if the rope wants to slide in this direction, wants to start slipping, that the frictional forces in these little pieces here are all in this direction... all the way around. And therefore, it helps T1, so to speak, and so because of the friction, which is opposing T2, T2 can now become much larger than T1. What you have to do to calculate this analytically, you have to evaluate these individual frictional forces for these very small slices, so that becomes an integral, and then you have to integrate it over an angle, which I will call theta zero. And I remember when I last lectured 801, that was in 1993, I derived that in class-- the relation between T2 and T1 as a function of this angle theta. And that took me about five minutes, and after five minutes, half the students were asleep. Now, I'm not sure whether you want to sleep five minutes, but I don't think, frankly, that you deserve it, so I decided to not do the derivation but to refer you to the book, which is page 361, and you will find, then, that in the situation that the rope wants to start slipping in this direction, that T2 divided by T1 is e to the power mu times theta zero, if the friction coefficient here is mu-- it would be the static friction coefficient. So that is the result. And notice that it is independent of the radius of this bar, which is not so obvious, strangely enough. Whether the bar is this small or this small makes no difference. It only depends on this angle. This angle could be very large. You could wrap it around ten times, as we will do very shortly. So there's no restriction on theta. If there were no friction at all, notice then at the moment that it starts to slip, e to the power 0 is 1, that's when T2 equals T1, that's obvious, so you can't play this game if mu is zero. You need friction. That is at the heart of this whole problem. And so now let's put in some numbers so that we get an idea of what we gain. So let us take the situation that we take let's say three turns. We wrap the rope around three times. So three turns. That means theta zero equals six pi. And let the friction coefficient mu be one-fifth, 0.2. So e to the power mu times theta zero is now about 40. So what that means is that with a force on this side which is 40 times smaller than a force on that side, I have a balanced situation. I can hold this in my hand and counter-- if you want to think of it that way-- a force on this side which is 40 times larger. But if I take six turns, then e to the power mu theta zero will be about 2,000-- 2,000! So now I can really control an elephant. Imagine now that I have here an object, capital M, which would be 5,000 kilograms... make it 10,000 kilograms. That's what I'm hanging here. I can hang here now a mass which is 2,000 times less massive. That means I could hang there five kilograms. And the tension here would be 2,000 times less than the tension there. The system would be about to start slipping, but it's not slipping. There is complete balance. So imagine I hold this part in my hand-- here is this 10,000-kilogram weight and I hold this in my hand. All I need is a force of about 50 newtons, and I'm standing there, and on the other side is this 10,000-kilogram weight. And now I just make my force a little less than 50 newtons, and what will happen now, it will start to slip. Remember? Because we calculated here the requirement for just not slipping. So now I let it go, and then the 10,000-kilogram on the other side will slowly go down. I can just control it, and I can stop it, and I can control it with a very, very small force. Now comes a question for you. Suppose I wanted to raise the 10,000-kilogram. Could I now pull a little bit more than 50 newtons? Just a teeny weeny little bit more? Would then that 10,000-kilogram come up? I see people shake their heads. Who thinks it would come up? Who thinks "no way"? Who doesn't think at all? Most of the people. sorry, I didn't mean to be nasty. Um... there's no way that you could pull it up, because if you want to pull this up, the situation completely reverses. Those frictional forces for this rope to go in this direction will flip over. In other words, what is now T1 in our calculations will become T2. So if you want this side to go down, if you want to have it slip in this direction, you're going to have that T1 divided by T2 is now e to the power mu theta zero. And so now if you have 10,000 kilograms here and if you had six turns, then the force that you need here is 2,000 times larger than 10,000 kilograms, and so you would need 20 million kilograms. So it would be the dumbest thing to do to lift it. You don't want to lift it. You use this device only to balance a very strong force-- that's the way it's used by sailors-- and even to control it, because you can slowly release it, and then the force on the other side will start to move. So you saw in a striking example of where friction helped us to balance, and now I want to discuss an object that is hanging and that can freely swing due to gravity. I want you to appreciate the situation of static equilibrium that the sum of all forces and the sum of all torques have to be zero. So here is this object which I'm going to hang, maybe on the wall, and here is a point P, and there is a... let's say a frictionless spin that I put in the wall, so the object can freely swing around. And if there is a torque relative to point P, it would be r P cross mg. It's clearly going to rotate about that point P. The torque equals I about that point P times alpha, and alpha is the angular acceleration. How can we ever get a stable situation? Nature is now going to think hard and is going to say, "Gee, I can only have stable equilibrium "if the sum of all forces equals zero "and if the sum of all torques relative to any point equals zero." And nature knows how to do that. That always will happen if P and the center of mass are along a vertical line. Because if that's the case, then there will be here a force mg, and then here there will be a force mg upwards. This object is hanging on that pin, so the pin-- action equals minus reaction-- will push upwards. So the sum of all forces is zero, and there is no torque. Take any point you want to-- this point or that point or this point or that point or that point-- there is no torque anymore. And so now there is complete equilibrium. So if you had an object, for instance, that looked like this, a thin rod-- there's a very massive object here, so that the center of mass is almost there-- then this situation would be stable. Or you might say this situation, because now we have the center of mass, mP, vertical line. So also here we have now mg down, so we have at the pin mg up. Sum of all forces is zero, sum of all torques is zero. If you just blow on this a little, it will swing over, it will start to rotate, whereas this is stable. If I move this to the side, it will come back to that position. But the basic idea is, then, that the center of mass will always in stable configuration line itself up below the point of suspension. I have here a triangle, and I have no clue where the center of mass is. It may be somewhere here. It may even be in open space, I don't know. So that the center of mass lines itself up precisely below my finger, so I can now take a pencil and draw a line here, or I can have a little string, and now I can change my finger to here and I can again draw this line, and where the two intersect is the center of mass. So the center of mass will always find itself below the point of suspension. Title: How to Be Anointed in Christianity Introduction: In Christian belief, being anointed refers to receiving divine grace or empowerment through the Holy Spirit. This concept is rooted in biblical traditions where anointing was often associated with priests, prophets, and kings as a symbol of their consecration to God's service. Here are some steps to help you understand what it means to be anointed in a spiritual sense within Christianity, including practical applications and key considerations. Step 1: Understand the Concept of Anointing The term 'anointing' comes from the Hebrew word "mashach" which means 'to smear or rub oil'. It represented consecration, dedication, or appointment by God for specific tasks. The New Testament Greek equivalent is "chrio", meaning 'to pour over', emphasizing the outpouring of the Holy Spirit upon individuals. Key Tip: Remember that while physical anointing with oil can accompany this spiritual experience, true anointing ultimately signifies a state of closeness with God, filled with His spirit. Step 2: Study Biblical Examples of Anointing Numerous figures in the Bible were anointed including Moses, Aaron, David, Saul, Elijah, Elisha, and Jesus himself during his baptism. Each instance carries unique significance but common themes include commissioning for ministry, healing, deliverance, or leadership. Guideline: Regularly reading and reflecting on these stories will deepen your understanding of anointing and inspire you towards seeking such experiences. Step 3: Pray for Anointing Asking God for more of His presence and power should be central to our pursuit of anointing. Prayer opens up channels for us to receive directly from Him. You might pray something like, "Lord, fill me with Your Holy Spirit so I may serve You effectively." Key Tip: Patience is vital here; remember that God works according to His perfect timing. Step 4: Surrender Control An essential part of being anointed involves yielding control to God. This requires humility and trust, recognizing that we cannot fulfill our purpose without His guidance and strength. Step 5: Serve Others Ministry opportunities provide fertile ground for experiencing anointing. As you reach out to others in love and service, expect God to work through you, making room for miraculous interventions. Step 6: Practice Righteous Living Scripture teaches that righteous living attracts divine favor. Therefore, striving to live a life pleasing to God increases your chances of being anointed. Step 7: Receive Communion Frequently According to Catholic doctrine, sacraments like communion offer graces that aid spiritual growth and intimacy with God - elements crucial for experiencing anointing. Step 8: Participate in Church Activities Active involvement in church activities exposes one to teachings and practices that foster spiritual maturity necessary for being anointed. Plus, group prayers and worship services create atmospheres ripe for experiencing the move of the Holy Spirit. Conclusion: Becoming anointed in Christianity isn't about achieving some lofty status, but rather surrendering fully to God, allowing him to equip, guide, and lead us into impactful ministries. By following these steps and maintaining a close relationship with God, believers position themselves to operate under the influence of the Holy Spirit, thereby becoming vessels fit for God's service. Today, let's talk about a fun concept called "pairwise disjoint," also known as "mutually disjoint" or "non-intersecting." This idea helps us understand when different groups of things don't have any members in common. Imagine you have three boxes - one with apples, another with oranges, and the last one with both apples and oranges. Are these boxes pairwise disjoint? First, let's define our term: two groups (or sets) are pairwise disjoint if they don't share ANY members when they're not identical. In other words, there should be no overlap between them. For example, going back to our boxes: 1. Box 1 has only red apples . 2. Box 2 has only orange oranges . 3. Box 3 has some yellow apples and purple oranges (yes, those exist in our imaginary world!). So, which pairs of boxes are pairwise disjoint here? Let's check: - Box 1 and Box 2: None of the fruits appear in both boxes, so they're pairwise disjoint . * Red apples Orange oranges . - Box 1 and Box 3: Although both boxes have apples, none of the same apples are present; hence, they're still pairwise disjoint . * Red apples Yellow apples . - Box 2 and Box 3: Unfortunately, this time around, we find overlapping items - purple oranges appear in both boxes . That means they're NOT pairwise disjoint. To sum up, pairwise disjointness tells us whether different collections (sets) contain unique elements without sharing anything among them. Remembering this will make it easier to sort out your toys, cards, stickers, or even cookies next time! Have fun exploring more mathematical ideas . Title: How to Perform an Impressive Trick with a Single Match Introduction: Performing tricks with ordinary objects can be a fun way to entertain yourself or others. With just one match, you can create an illusion that will leave your audience amazed! This tutorial provides clear, detailed instructions on executing this trick successfully. By following these steps closely and practicing regularly, you'll soon master this neat party piece. Materials Needed: - A single wooden matchstick (preferably longer than regular ones) Step 1: Choose the Right Match Select a sturdy, unbroken wooden matchstick free from cracks or bends. Longer matches are easier to handle due to their increased surface area. Avoid using safety matches as they contain extra phosphorus which may cause unwanted ignition during the trick. Rationale: Using the right type of match ensures safety and makes the trick more manageable. It also reduces the risk of failure caused by weak or improper materials. Step 2: Hold the Match Correctly Grasp the match firmly between your thumb and index finger, close to its head but without obstructing the striking surface. Your grip should allow easy access to both ends while maintaining control over the match. Guideline: Practice holding the match securely yet comfortably to prevent dropping it accidentally. Key Tip: Keep your fingernails short to avoid damaging the match when gripping it tightly. Step 3: Strike the Match Strike the match against any rough surface until it sparks and lights up. Once lit, hold it away from your body and other flammable items. Blow gently on the flame if necessary to keep it alight. Explanation: Properly lighting the match is crucial for performing the subsequent steps smoothly. Familiarize yourself with different striking surfaces to find what works best for you. Step 4: Balance the Lit Match Upright Place the burning end onto a flat, non-flammable surface such as a table or countertop. Slowly lift the bottom part of the match and balance it vertically so that only the lit tip touches the surface. Be patient; achieving stability might take several attempts. Purpose: The goal here is to create the impression that the match remains upright solely through the force of the flame, defying gravity. Key Tips: - Use a steady hand and minimal movements to maintain balance. - Find a perfectly horizontal surface to make balancing easier. Step 5: Pretend to Remove Support While carefully observing the balanced match, place your free hand near it as if preparing to remove the supporting surface beneath. However, do not actually touch or move anything. Maintain eye contact with the match throughout this process. Reason: Creating suspense adds intrigue to the trick, making the final reveal more impactful. Step 6: Reveal the Secret As you bring your hand closer to the match, quickly tilt the match towards your fingers, revealing that there was never any support needed - the lit end simply adheres to your skin due to capillary action. Explain this phenomenon briefly before extinguishing the match safely. Conclusion: Mastering this trick requires patience and practice. Remember to always prioritize safety and respect fire regulations. Enjoy impressing friends and family with your newfound skill! But we don't put tomatoes on it! Title: How to Clap with One Hand Introduction: While clapping typically requires two hands, it is possible to create a similar sound using just one hand. This technique can be useful in situations where you only have one free hand or want to make a unique impression. However, keep in mind that mastering this skill may require practice and patience. Here's our comprehensive guide on how to clap with one hand. Step 1: Position Your Hand Hold your dominant hand upright, fingers together, and thumb extended. Keep your wrist loose but stable. This position allows for maximum control over the movement required for clapping. Key Tip: Maintain flexibility in your wrist; stiffness will reduce power and precision. Step 2: Form a Cup Shape Curl your fingers slightly towards your palm so they form an arc shape resembling a shallow cup. The exact curvature depends on personal comfort and preferred sound volume. A more pronounced curve generally produces louder sounds. Guideline: Avoid pressing too hard against your palm as this could cause discomfort or even injury. Step 3: Strike the Palm Quickly thrust your thumb downward onto the finger-cup while simultaneously snapping your wrist upwards. This motion should produce a sharp slap noise reminiscent of traditional clapping. Important Note: Timing is crucial here. Both actions (thumb strike and wrist snap) must occur almost simultaneously for optimal effect. Step 4: Adjust Volume and Sound Quality Experiment with different angles, speeds, and pressures until you find what works best for you. Practice makes perfect! Remember, there isn't a single correct way to do this - everyone develops their own unique method based on factors like hand size, strength, and flexibility. Helpful Hint: Using lotion before practicing might help prevent skin irritation from frequent friction. Step 5: Use in Context Now that you know how to perform a one-handed clap, try incorporating it into real-life scenarios. It might feel strange at first, but remember, confidence plays a significant role in pulling off such unconventional techniques. Precautions: Although fun and impressive when done right, repeated one-handed claps can lead to blisters, calluses, or sore muscles, especially during initial stages of learning. Take regular breaks, stay hydrated, and listen to your body. If any pain persists, consult a healthcare professional. Conclusion: With consistent effort and dedication, anyone can learn to clap with one hand effectively. Aside from being a neat party trick, honing this ability also promotes coordination, focus, and fine motor skills development. So go ahead, give it a shot, and show off your newfound talent proudly! Kanji is an adaptation of the Chinese logographic writing system, meaning each symbol was originally meant to look like the word it was describing. Each symbol can represent a syllable (eg. - dai) or a word (eg. - oto (sound)). There are multiple pronunciations for each Kanji character, categorised into On'yomi ( ) or Kun'yomi ( ), depending on the word's origin. Mommy how we going to get dose wheels on here? The UK's constitution is not written in one single document like many other countries' are. In fact, the UK's constitution is not completely written down at all. Some of it can be found in writing, starting with the Magna Carta of 1215 and the Bill of Rights Act 1689 and including more modern Acts of Parliament. Other parts of it are considered common law and are made up of the decisions of judges over many hundreds of years in a system called legal or judicial precedence. Because of this, some people say that the United Kingdom has a "de facto" or "unwritten" constitution. Can they [crawfish/lobsters] be in the beach too? This means that the creditors agree to take less money to discharge their debts. There are similar processes for companies and other organisations, and they go by various different names in different countries, but in many countries they are called "schemes of arrangement". You're sneezing a lot. Your friend asks you what's wrong. You think you're sneezing because of your allergies. You say: There must be a lot of pollen. When you see evidence of something, or you have a strong reason to believe something, you can say "There must be ___". For example, when you hear music playing and people talking in the house next door to you, you can say: There must be a party going on. If your toilet doesn't flush correctly, you can say: There must be something stuck in there. Some English learners use the word "much" too much! In normal conversational English, "a lot of ___" is far more common than "much ___": There was a lot of dust in the air. My daughter always likes to put a lot of ketchup on her fries. However, you can use "much" when you're saying "not much": There's not much money left. And it's good in the phrase "too much": Sorry, I can't. I have way too much work to do here. You can also use "much" for very, very formal positive sentences. When I hear a sentence like this, it reminds me of the Bible! It has that level of formality and age. There was much rejoicing among the people. "Many" is the same as "much". It sounds strange and old-fashioned in positive sentences, but normal in questions and negative sentences. There were a lot of people there. There aren't very many chairs left. "Pollen" is the dust that flowers produce. A lot of people are allergic to pollen. Title: How to Be the Best Big Sister You Can =========================================== Introduction: Being a big sister is an exciting and rewarding experience! It comes with great responsibilities but also provides countless opportunities for growth, bonding, and making cherished memories together. This wikiHow will guide you through various steps to become the best big sister you can be, ensuring a harmonious and loving relationship between you and your little sibling(s). ### Step 1: Set a Positive Example Explanation: As a big sister, you have the power to significantly influence your younger siblings' behavior, values, and self-perception. By setting a positive example, you help shape their character and encourage them to develop good habits. Key Tips: * Demonstrate kindness, compassion, honesty, and respect towards others. Your actions speak louder than words. * Pursue hobbies and interests that promote personal development and wellbeing, such as reading, sports, or arts. Encourage your siblings to join you in these activities when appropriate. ### Step 2: Foster Open Communication Explanation: An open and honest dialogue fosters trust, understanding, and emotional intelligence within families. When you communicate effectively with your siblings, you create a safe space where they feel comfortable sharing thoughts, feelings, and concerns. Key Tips: * Listen actively by paying full attention to what your siblings say without interrupting them. Validate their emotions and opinions even if you disagree. * Share age-appropriate information about yourself and your experiences. Letting your siblings into your world encourages them to do the same. * Ask open-ended questions rather than yes/no queries to prompt more thoughtful responses from your siblings. ### Step 3: Provide Guidance and Support Explanation: Offering guidance and support enables you to play a significant role in shaping your siblings' lives while strengthening your bond. Helping them navigate challenges reinforces your position as a trusted confidant and mentor. Key Tips: * Identify teachable moments during daily interactions to impart knowledge, skills, or advice gently. Avoid being overbearing or condescending. * Offer assistance only when asked unless there's an immediate risk or danger involved. Respect your siblings' autonomy and allow them to learn from their mistakes. * Celebrate achievements and milestones together. Show genuine interest in their accomplishments and share in their joy. ### Step 4: Establish Healthy Boundaries What if I crack something cut something in half? Introducing a song book to your students is a great way to help develop their speaking and listening skills. Each week the students will learn a new song to sing and recite. They will learn the words, actions and also complete a craft to reinforce the key concept/s of the song. The first song we learnt was 'If you're happy and you know it'. This song was introduced whilst we were learning about feelings. After learning the original version the students discussed different ways to move/act to different feelings e.g. If you're angry and you know it stamp your feet, If you're excited and you know it high five a friend. We also completed a craft to match the song. This will be displayed in each students song book next to the song print out. To create the craft the students first made handprints in paint. They then looked through magazines to find facial features of happy people, and glued these onto a head template. Finally the students coloured in the t-shirts on the head templates and added wool for hair. I then displayed the pictures together under the title 'If you're happy and you know it'. Each letter in the word happy is presented on a patterned paper plate. I also added a description of the task to the display so that when the parents are looking at their childs work they have a better understanding of the task. A runner can be put out by tagging the runner while the runner is not on a base, and by "forcing him out" (when a base is touched before a player can get there, with no base for the runner to go back to). When the fielding team has put out three of the batting team's players, the "half-inning" is over and the team in the field and the team at bat switch places. Okay you've got your piggies up there. Title: How to Perform a Cartwheel for Cheerleading Performing a cartwheel is an essential skill for cheerleaders. It demonstrates strength, flexibility, and control - all crucial elements of successful cheerleading. This step-by-step guide will take you through the process of executing a perfect cartwheel, providing detailed explanations and helpful tips along the way. **Step 1: Preparation** Before attempting a cartwheel, ensure that you have enough space around you to perform the move safely without hitting any objects or people. Practice on a soft surface like grass if possible. Warm up properly by stretching your arms, shoulders, wrists, back, hips, and legs. *Why this step matters*: Proper preparation reduces the risk of injury and ensures your body is ready for physical activity. **Step 2: Stance and Arm Position** Stand with your feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent. Extend both arms straight above your head, palms facing forward. Your fingers should be together and thumbs overlapping or touching. This hand position provides stability during the rotation. *Key tip*: Keep your eyes focused on where you want to land throughout the entire cartwheel. **Step 3: Starting Position** Lunge forward with one foot (let's say the right), placing most of your weight onto it while keeping the left leg relatively straight but bend at the knee slightly. Both hands should still be raised high in the air. *Guideline*: The leg further from the direction you are turning (left leg) should remain fairly straight, acting as a pivot point. **Step 4: First Half of the Rotation** Push off forcefully with your planted foot (right foot). Simultaneously swing both arms down and backward so they end up behind your hips, creating momentum. As you do this, lift your left leg up and over your body, kicking it out straight as soon as it clears your head. At this stage, you should look like a human wheel spinning in mid-air. *Why this step works*: Swinging your arms and pushing off your standing foot generate power and speed needed for the flip. Lifting your non-supporting leg early allows for better balance and control during the second half of the rotation. **Step 5: Second Half of the Rotation** As your first leg comes down, plant it firmly on the ground. Continue swinging your arms upward until they reach their original starting position overhead. Land on the ball of your supporting foot, then roll onto your whole foot as you complete the turn. Your trailing leg should also make contact with the ground simultaneously with your hands. *Key tip*: Absorb the impact of landing by bending your knees upon touchdown. **Step 6: Finishing Position** End the cartwheel with your feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent, and arms extended upward once again. Remember to keep your gaze fixed on your intended landing spot throughout the maneuver. *Why this final pose matters*: Completing the cartwheel with good form showcases mastery of the skill and readiness for subsequent movements. Practice these steps regularly to build confidence and improve technique. With patience and persistence, you'll soon be able to execute flawless cartwheels that add flair and excitement to your cheerleading performances! Creating and using folders for apps on your iPhone can help you organize your home screen, making it easier to find the apps you need and reducing clutter. This tutorial will guide you through the process of creating and managing app folders on your iPhone. **Step 1: Preparation** Before beginning, ensure that your iPhone is updated to the latest version of iOS. To check for updates, go to Settings > General > Software Update. If an update is available, follow the prompts to install it. Updating your iPhone ensures that you have access to all the latest features and bug fixes related to organizing apps into folders. **Step 2: Select Apps for Organizing** Identify which apps you want to group together in a folder. For example, if you often use social media apps like Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, and LinkedIn, consider placing them in one folder labeled "Social Media." Grouping similar apps makes finding what you need more efficient. **Step 3: Create a New Folder** To create a new folder, touch and hold any app icon until all apps start jiggling (this indicates edit mode). Then, drag one app onto another app you wish to include in the same folder. Release your finger once both apps overlap slightly; this action creates a new folder containing those two apps. A default name based on the category or genre of these apps appears atop the folder. Key Tip: You cannot add more than 16 apps to a single folder without first opening it. So plan accordingly when deciding how many apps should be placed within each folder. **Step 4: Edit the Folder Name** Tap on the suggested folder name to activate editing mode, then type over it with your preferred label. Keep folder names short yet descriptive so they remain legible on smaller screens. When finished naming the folder, press the 'Done' button on the keyboard or tap anywhere outside the text field. Guideline: Avoid abbreviating words too much as it may make the labels confusing or difficult to understand quickly. **Step 5: Add More Apps to the Folder** While still in edit mode, continue adding additional apps by dragging their icons into the newly created folder. Arrange them inside alphabetically, categorically, or according to usage frequency - whichever method works best for you. Once satisfied with the organization, press the Home button or swipe up from the bottom edge of the display (on newer iPhones) to exit edit mode. Key Tip: To remove an app from a folder, open the folder, touch and hold any app icon until they jiggle again, then drag the unwanted app out of the folder. **Step 6: Manage Existing Folders** Over time, you might find that certain folders become unnecessary or could benefit from reorganization. Follow steps 3-5 above to modify existing folders. Remember that renaming or changing the contents of a folder doesn't affect its position on the home screen(s), so manually move it if needed. Guideline: It's recommended to limit the number of folders you create to maintain simplicity and ease of navigation. Too many folders can lead to confusion and counteract the benefits gained from better app organization. By following these steps, you can effectively create and manage app folders on your iPhone, improving overall user experience and efficiency. I think she was saying something about me. A number of security flaws affecting IE originated not in the browser itself, but ActiveX-based add-ons used by it. Because the add-ons have the same privilege as IE, the flaws can be as critical as browser flaws. This has led to the ActiveX-based architecture being criticized for being fault-prone. By 2005, some experts maintained that the dangers of ActiveX have been overstated and there were safeguards in place. In 2006, new techniques using automated testing found more than a hundred vulnerabilities in standard Microsoft ActiveX components. Here's an attempt to explain the concept of continuity using grade-school level language and examples: --- Have you ever tried drawing a smooth curve without picking up your pencil? If you have, then congratulations - you've demonstrated one of the fundamental concepts in mathematics known as continuity! Continuity basically means that if we draw a tiny arrow around a point on our curve, then the tip of the arrow will always end up close to some point on the curve. In other words, small changes in input (the position where we put the arrow) lead to small changes in output (where the arrow ends up). This idea applies to all sorts of functions, not just curves. Now let's consider a strange rule for turning numbers into new numbers: if the number is a fraction or a decimal with repeating digits (which mathematicians call "rational"), leave it alone; otherwise, change it to zero ("set it equal to zero", in math speak). We write this rule as follows: $$f(x)= \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} x,& x{\text~{\textsf{s a rational number}}}\\ 0,& x {\text~{\textsf{isn't a rational number}}} \end{array} \right.$$ Here's where things get interesting. Is this rule continuous? To answer this question, imagine putting our little arrow around any nonzero number. On one side of our arrow, we may find fractions like 1/2 or 3/4. These guys won't change under our rule since they're rational numbers. But on the other side, we might encounter numbers like pi or sqrt(2), which do change to zero when we apply our rule. So even though both types of numbers lie within our tiny region, their outputs don't stay near each other after applying our rule! And that violates our definition of continuity. Therefore, despite being able to draw its graph without lifting our pen, this peculiar function isn't actually continuous. It has sudden jumps whenever we cross over from rational to irrational numbers. Quite fascinating, huh? The sailors, however, had now made a very useful weapon: they had procured some large, heavy stones, which they had sown up in canvas and fastened to long ropes. These they threw on the Zulus, and stunned or sunk them. A girl named um Rachel saw some aliens come out and an alien dog with no eyes. This is the alphabet up there, huh? [Nina starts dancing around and resisting her mother's attempts to put on suspenders.] Title: How to Decorate Your Graduation Cap Introduction: Graduating from high school or college is an exciting milestone that deserves celebration! One way to make your graduation day even more special is by decorating your graduation cap. This can be a fun and creative way to express yourself, showcase your personality, or honor any achievements, clubs, or organizations you were involved with during your academic journey. Here's our comprehensive guide on how to decorate your graduation cap like a pro. Things You'll Need: - A clean, dry graduation cap - Acrylic paint (various colors) - Paintbrushes (various sizes) - Adhesive spray or hot glue gun - Glitter or rhinestones (optional) - Printed designs or stencils (optional) - Clear sealant spray (optional) Step 1: Cleaning Your Cap Before starting the decoration process, ensure your cap is free from dirt, dust, or oils by gently wiping it down with a damp cloth. Allow the cap to air dry completely before moving onto the next step. This ensures proper adherence of paint and embellishments. Step 2: Planning Your Design Brainstorm ideas about what you want to put on your cap. It could be anything from your name and major, inspirational quotes, personal mantras, favorite pop culture references, logos of sports teams or companies, or symbols representing hobbies and interests. Sketch out your design using pencil and paper until you are satisfied with its layout and appearance. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Keep it simple - intricate details might get lost at a distance. - Consider legibility - viewers should be able to read your message clearly when standing several feet away. - Be mindful of color contrast - dark text on light backgrounds works best for visibility. - Respect copyright laws if incorporating brand logos into your design. Step 3: Applying Base Coats If necessary, apply base coats of acrylic paint to create solid colored areas within your design. For example, if planning to write white letters over a black area, first cover the designated space with black paint. Make sure to let these base coats fully dry before proceeding. Step 4: Painting Letters and Details Using a thin brush, carefully outline your design elements with a steady hand. Fill them in as needed using larger brushes for broader strokes. If making mistakes, allow the paint to dry then lightly scrape off excess with a fingernail or toothpick without damaging the surface below. Step 5: Adding Embellishments (Optional) Add some sparkle to your cap with glitter or rhinestones! Apply adhesive spray or hot glue sparingly to the backside of your chosen embellishment, place it where desired, and hold firmly in position until set. Repeat this process until all intended embellishments have been added. Step 6: Sealing Your Creation (Optional) Protect your artwork from smudges, scratches, and weather conditions by applying clear sealant spray. Hold the can approximately eight inches away from the cap while spraying evenly across the entire decorated surface. Allow the sealant to dry thoroughly according to manufacturer instructions before wearing. Conclusion: Now that you've followed our detailed steps on how to decorate your graduation cap, take pride in knowing you created something unique and memorable. Share photos of your masterpiece online, wear it proudly during commencement ceremonies, and cherish it as a keepsake commemorating your achievement. Congratulations graduate! After gaining independence in 1991 with the end of the Soviet Union, Azerbaijan made the long and difficult change from a command economy to a market economy. The government has largely completed privatization of agricultural lands and small, medium and large state-owned companies. Azerbaijan is continuing making economic reforms, and old economic ties and structures have been slowly replaced. With independence, Azerbaijan became a member of the International Monetary Fund, the World Bank, the European Bank for Reconstruction and Development, the Islamic Development Bank and the Asian Development Bank. Azerbaijan's currency is the Azerbaijani manat (AZN) which is divided into 100 q pik. It became the national currency in 1992 and replaced the old Soviet ruble. The Central Bank of Azerbaijan was created in 1992. The Central Bank serves as Azerbaijan's central bank, and is responsible for printing and distributing the national currency, the Azerbaijani manat, and to control all commercial banks. Title: "K-Means Clustering with Percentage Data: A Fun Grade School Explanation!" Imagine you have a big bag full of jelly beans of different colors. Now, let's say you want to organize these jelly beans into smaller bags based on their colors without counting each individual jelly bean. Instead, you decide to count the proportion or percentage of each color compared to the total number of jelly beans. This way, you can create groups of similar colored jelly beans using percentages! That's exactly what k-means clustering on percentage data is all about! First, let's understand what k-means clustering means. Imagine cutting out circles from a piece of paper - some small, some large. These circles represent the "clusters," and our goal is to place similar items (jelly beans!) inside each circle. To determine which jelly beans belong together, we calculate the distance between every jelly bean and the center of each circle. The jelly beans then join the nearest circle or cluster. Now, when working with numbers like percentages, sometimes applying k-means clustering could be tricky because these values may vary greatly in size. So, to make sure no single value influences the outcome more than others due to its larger magnitude, we adjust or normalize these percentages to fall within a specific range-usually between 0 and 1. It's just like resizing pictures so they fit better on a page; everything has equal importance now! But why should we trust k-means clustering with our beloved jelly beans? Well, before deciding whether to group our jelly beans using percentages, we must examine the data visually, consult experts (or friends who love analyzing candies too!), and test other methods to ensure we get the best results. After all, nobody wants unevenly distributed jelly beans! So remember, next time you find yourself surrounded by mixed jelly beans or need to sort through lots of information involving percents, think of k-means clustering as a fun and fair approach to organizing things effectively! According as the people of _Provence_ do report, there dwelt sometime in that jurisdiction, two noble Knights, each well possessed of Castles & followers; the one beeing named _Messer Guiglielmo de Rossiglione_, and the other _Messer Guiglielmo Guardastagno_. Now, in regard that they were both valiant Gentlemen, and singularly expert in actions of Armes; they loved together the more mutually, and held it as a kinde of custom, to be seene in all Tiltes and Tournaments, or any other exercises of Armes, going commonly alike in their wearing garments. And although their Castles stood about five miles distant each from other, yet were they dayly conversant together, as very loving and intimate friends. The one of them, I meane _Messer Guiglielmo de Rossiglione_, had to wife a very gallant beautifull Lady, of whom _Messer Guardastagno_ (forgetting the lawes of respect and loyall friendshippe) became over-fondly enamoured, expressing the same by such outward meanes, that the Lady her selfe tooke knowledge thereof, and not with any dislike, as it seemed, but rather lovingly entertained; yet she grew not so forgetfull of her honour and estimation, as the other did of faith to his friend. With such indiscretion was this idle love carried, that whether it sorted to effect, or no, I know not: but the husband perceived some such manner of behaviour, as hee could not easily digest, nor thought it fitting to endure. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/Parent Child/SLI/PCs/sno42.cha = = = [holding up tube of play shaving cream?] Did you tell Urs'la where you went yesterday afternoon? oh, Erin! looks at all these toys. They had been concerned that science textbooks in junior high school had so many technical words. They felt that teachers spent all class time explaining their meaning. Title: How to Get a Friend Finally Introduction: Making friends can be challenging, especially if you're new to an area or have recently gone through significant life changes. However, with patience, persistence, and the right mindset, you can form meaningful friendships that will enrich your life. This comprehensive guide offers actionable steps and valuable insights into expanding your social circle and building strong connections. Step 1: Improve Your Self-Confidence * Explanation: A solid foundation of self-confidence is crucial when meeting potential friends because people are generally more attracted to confident individuals. When you believe in yourself, you project positivity, which makes others feel comfortable around you. * Key Tips & Guidelines: + Practice positive affirmations daily. + Set achievable goals and celebrate accomplishments. + Engage in activities that make you feel good about yourself. + Learn from constructive criticism without internalizing negativity. Step 2: Identify Common Interests * Explanation: Discovering shared hobbies and passions increases the likelihood of forming genuine bonds since common ground facilitates conversation and fosters mutual understanding. * Key Tips & Guidelines: + Make a list of your favorite pastimes. Title: How to Delete Forwarded Emails on a MacBook Deleting forwarded emails on a MacBook can help you manage your inbox and keep it organized. This tutorial will guide you through the process of deleting forwarded emails using Apple's built-in Mail app. Follow these steps carefully to ensure that all unwanted messages are removed from your mailbox. **Step 1:** **Open the Mail App** * Launch the Mail application by clicking its icon on the Dock or searching for it via Spotlight Search (accessible by pressing Command + Space). The Mail app is preinstalled on every MacBook, so there's no need to download any additional software. **Step 2:** **Select the Account** * Once the Mail app is open, choose the account where the forwarded email resides. If you have multiple email accounts linked to your Mail app, they will be listed along the left sidebar under "Mailboxes." Click on the desired account. **Step 3:** **Choose the Folder** * After selecting an account, navigate to the folder containing the forwarded email(s) you wish to delete. Common folders include "Inbox," "Sent," and "Trash." Keep in mind that deleted emails aren't permanently erased until you empty the Trash folder. **Key Tip:** *If you cannot find the forwarded email, try using the search bar at the top right corner of the window to locate the message.* **Step 4:** **Locate the Forwarded Email** * Scroll through the list of emails within the chosen folder until you find the forwarded message(s). Messages marked with an arrow symbol pointing right usually indicate that an email has been forwarded. **Step 5:** **Select the Forwarded Email** * To select a single email, simply click on it once. You may also select multiple consecutive emails by holding down the Shift key while clicking on them, or nonconsecutive emails by pressing the Command key while clicking. **Step 6:** **Delete the Selected Email(s)** * With the forwarded email(s) selected, press the "Delete" key on your keyboard or right-click (or control-click) and choose "Move to Trash." Alternatively, you can utilize the "Delete" button located in the toolbar above the message list. **Key Guideline:** *Be cautious when deleting emails as this action cannot be undone without restoring from a backup or manually moving the email back to its original location.* **Step 7:** **Empty the Trash Folder (Optional)** * If you want to permanently remove the deleted email(s), right-click (or control-click) the "Trash" entry in the left sidebar and then select "Erase Deleted Items." Confirm the operation if prompted. Please note that this step is optional; otherwise, the emails will remain in the Trash folder until manually emptied or after 30 days. By following these steps, you should now have successfully deleted one or more forwarded emails from your MacBook's Mail app. Remember to double-check before deleting any important messages, as this action is irreversible without proper precautions. We all know how important this skill is in promoting number sense in young learners! In this game called "I have Who Has ?" for grades 1 and 2, students start with a card (or more than one, if there are enough cards to go around!). Choose any child you like to begin the cycle. She reads from her card, from left to right, saying the number represented in the picture ("I have 14.") and then asking the question ("Who has seven?"). Students listen for their number, then ask their question until all students have had a chance to read. The game is over when the first person to read, reads again. Be sure and hand out ALL the cards, or the cycle won't work I have created a Kindergarten version of the "I have Who has ?" game as well. The numbers go only to 10, and there are picture of fingers to help them "read" the number word. A small group - or partners - works best for this one, since there are only ten cards! Have fun with this! (And for my friends in Coquitlam who witnessed the spectacular initial fail of this game, rest assured that I've fixed it!) See if you miss one you height count em again. Title: How to Delete or Clear Saved FTP Passwords FTP (File Transfer Protocol) is a standard network protocol used for transferring computer files between a client and server on a computer network. Sometimes, you may need to delete or clear saved FTP passwords due to various reasons such as security concerns or when changing your credentials. This tutorial will guide you through the process step-by-step. **On Windows Systems:** 1. **Open File Explorer:** Click on the folder icon usually found in your taskbar or press `Windows Key + E`. 2. **Access Network Location View:** In the file explorer window, look at the top menu bar and click on 'Computer' or 'This PC', then select 'Map network drive'. A new dialog box will appear. Key Tip: If you don't see these options, right-click on 'Computer' or 'This PC' from the start menu and choose 'Map network drive'. = = = children/CHIDES_NA/Mawhinney/030018a.cha = = = Where did we go with Uncle Robert, Martin? Avoiding a crush can be challenging, especially if you have strong feelings for someone. However, taking steps to distance yourself emotionally and physically from that person can help prevent heartache and maintain your mental well-being. Here's an in-depth guide on how to avoid developing or maintaining a crush: ### Step 1: Acknowledge Your Feelings Recognize that you have a crush and accept it as a natural human emotion. Understanding and acknowledging your feelings is essential before you can take actionable steps to move past them. This self-awareness will enable you to make conscious decisions about engaging with the object of your affection. Key tip: Be honest with yourself; denying or suppressing your emotions may lead to more intense infatuation or unhealthy behaviors. ### Step 2: Identify Triggers Reflect upon what triggers your attraction towards this individual. Is it their personality, appearance, shared interests, or something else? By identifying these triggers, you can better understand why you developed a crush and learn to manage those triggers when they appear. Guideline: Keep track of situations where you find yourself thinking about the person excessively or feeling butterflies in your stomach. These instances could provide clues regarding what sparks your admiration. ### Step 3: Limit Interaction Reduce opportunities for interaction with the person who has become the focus of your affections. The less time spent together, the easier it becomes to shift your attention elsewhere. If necessary, create physical distance by avoiding places or events where you know they'll be present. Key tip: Politely decline invitations to social gatherings involving the person without drawing unnecessary attention to your actions. Explain that you already have plans or simply state that you are unable to attend. ### Step 4: Find Distractions Engage in activities that divert your attention away from the person. Pursue hobbies, join clubs, volunteer, or enroll in classes related to your interests. Not only will this keep you occupied, but also increase chances of meeting new people and expanding your social circle. Guideline: Make sure these distractions align with your values, passions, and personal growth goals. Engaging in meaningful pursuits can improve overall happiness and reduce preoccupation with the crush. ### Step 5: Practice Self-Care Maintaining good mental health is crucial during this process. Prioritize self-care through exercise, proper nutrition, meditation, and adequate sleep. These habits promote emotional stability and resilience, making it easier to navigate complex feelings associated with having a crush. Key tip: Develop a consistent self-care routine to ensure ongoing support for your emotional well-being. ### Step 6: Seek Support Share your concerns with trusted friends or family members. Talking openly about your situation can offer valuable insights, comfort, and encouragement. Additionally, discussing your feelings with others might shed light on factors contributing to your infatuation that you hadn't previously considered. Guideline: Choose confidants wisely, ensuring they have your best interest at heart and respect boundaries while offering guidance. ### Step 7: Reframe Thought Patterns Challenge any negative thought patterns surrounding the person or relationship. Instead of dwelling on idealized versions of reality, practice gratitude for positive aspects of your life. Focus on cultivating healthy friendships and relationships rather than fixating on one unattainable connection. Key tip: Implement cognitive behavioral techniques such as journaling, visualization exercises, or affirmations to reframe thoughts and develop a more balanced perspective. ### Step 8: Learn From Experience One two three four five six seven eight nine ten. Title: How to Fill Out a Deposit Slip A deposit slip is a form used by banks to process transactions where customers add money into their accounts. It contains information about the depositor, bank account number, and cash or checks being deposited. Filling out this document correctly ensures that your funds are accurately credited to your account, preventing any delays or errors. This article will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to fill out a deposit slip using a sample deposit slip as reference. **Things You'll Need:** 1. A blank deposit slip from your banking institution 2. Pen or pencil 3. Checks (if applicable) 4. Cash (if applicable) 5. Identification (for verification purposes when making large deposits at some financial institutions) **Step 1: Obtain Your Bank's Blank Deposit Slip** Most people get these slips from their checkbooks provided by their respective banks. However, if you do not have one available, they can usually be found near teller stations inside branches or even online through mobile apps or e-banking platforms. Always ensure you obtain an official deposit slip specific to your banking institution since formats may vary between different banks. **Step 2: Enter Today's Date** Write down today's date on the line indicated for it. Some deposit slips might already have the current date printed, so double-check before writing anything over it. Writing the correct date assists both you and the bank processor in tracking when the transaction occurred. **Step 3: Provide Account Information** Enter your full name or business name and complete checking or savings account number(s) associated with the deposit in their designated spaces. Make sure all numbers are accurate; otherwise, your deposit could go to another customer's account. **Step 4: Specify Cash vs Check Amounts** Divide your total deposit amount based on whether you're depositing currency (cash), checks, or both. Use separate lines for each category if given. If there isn't enough space, continue listing them below the dotted line, but remember to draw arrows pointing upwards towards the corresponding subtotal box. **Key Tips:** * When adding multiple checks, write the individual amounts clearly in ink to prevent misreading by automated machines. * Keep track of your running total while filling out the form to avoid mathematical errors later. **Step 5: Calculate Subtotals & Total Deposit Amount** Add together all the cash amounts and record the sum in the 'CASH' subtotal box. Do the same for the checks under the 'CHECKS' subtotal box. Finally, calculate the grand total by adding the two subtotals together and enter this figure in the 'TOTAL DEPOSIT' section. **Key Guidelines:** * Double-check your calculations to make sure everything adds up correctly. * Most modern banks now utilize magnetic ink character recognition technology, which makes handwriting legibility essential. **Step 6: Signature** Sign the deposit slip where required, typically at the bottom right corner. By signing, you confirm that the details filled out are true and accurate. Additionally, providing identification upon request during large deposits enhances security against potential fraudulent activities. **Key Tip:** * Remember, without proper authorization via signature, your bank won't accept responsibility for processing mistakes. By following these steps meticulously, you significantly reduce the risk of encountering issues related to incorrectly completed deposit slips, thus ensuring smooth processing of your hard-earned money. Happy banking! Infections of the ear can affect one or more of the various parts of the ear. There are three main parts to the ear: inner ear, middle ear and outer ear. The outer ear collects sounds, and consists of the ear and ear canal up to the eardrum. The middle ear is the space beneath the ear drum and is about the size of a pencil eraser. The middle ear is an air-filled space that is separated from the outer ear by a very thin eardrum. Attached to the eardrum are three bones. Sound waves hit the eardrum and make it vibrate. The eardrum vibrations make the attached bones transmit the vibrations to the inner ear. The inner ear converts the vibrations to electrical signals and sends these signals to the brain. The inner ear also helps maintain your physical balance. The Eustachian tube connects the middle ear space to the nasopharynx in the back of the nasal cavity. This tube helps equalize ear pressure. No not on the table on the paper! Title: How to Build a Coffin Introduction: This guide will walk you through the process of building a simple wooden coffin. This project is suitable for those with basic woodworking skills and tools. It's important to note that this tutorial is intended for decorative or educational purposes only. Always check local regulations regarding burial practices before proceeding. Materials Needed: 1. 1" x 8" x 6' boards (quantity: 5) 2. 1" x 4" x 8' board (quantity: 3) 3. Wood glue 4. Nails or screws (1 ") 5. Sandpaper (120 grit) 6. Paint or stain (optional) 7. Hinges (if lid is to be attached) Steps: Step 1: Cutting the Boards Cut one 1" x 8" x 6' board into two pieces, each 79 inches long (these will form the sides). Cut another board into four equal parts, each 24 inches long (these will become the ends and bottom). From the remaining three boards, cut six pieces at 19.5 inches (these will serve as the braces). Lastly, from the 1" x 4" board, cut four strips, each 79 inches long (these will be used for the lid). Key Tip: Measure twice, cut once! Accuracy in these measurements ensures a well-fitted coffin. Step 2: Assembling the Base Apply wood glue along the edge of one short piece (end) and attach it to the end of a long side piece using nails or screws. Repeat this process with another short piece on the other side. Now, take the last short piece and place it centrally under the open ends; this creates the bottom support. Secure it by applying glue and nailing it down from both sides. Guideline: Make sure all joints are square and tight for structural integrity. Step 3: Adding Braces Glue and nail the brace pieces diagonally inside the box at regular intervals. These braces strengthen the structure and prevent warping over time. Allow the adhesive to dry according to manufacturer's instructions. Key Tip: Pre-drill holes to prevent splitting of the wood when driving in the nails. Step 4: Creating the Lid Take the four 79 inch long strips of 1" x 4" board. Space them evenly apart on top of the base unit, creating an empty space slightly larger than the interior dimensions of your coffin. Screw or nail them securely onto the top edges of the base unit. Guideline: Remember to leave enough gap between the lid planks for expansion due to changes in humidity. Step 5: Finishing Touches Sand all surfaces until smooth. If desired, paint or stain the coffin now. Once dried, install hinges if attaching the lid permanently. Key Tip: Use non-toxic paints or stains if the coffin will be used for taxidermy or other animal related projects. Conclusion: Congratulations! You have successfully built a coffin. Whether it's for Halloween decoration, a DIY funeral, or simply a unique furniture piece, remember to always respect cultural sensitivities and legal requirements associated with such items.
