In botany, a fruit is a plant structure that contains the plant's seeds. To a botanist, the word "fruit" is used only if it comes from the part of the flower which was an ovary. It is an extra layer round the seeds, which may or may not be fleshy. However, even in the field of botany, there is no general agreement on how fruits should be classified. Many do have extra layers from other parts of the flower. [whispers (barely gets out the breath for utterance)] yeah, they have that shine to 'em I don't know like mercury? Who walks out through the back door? Nutrition in Early Child Years (For Ages 1 to 3) Children grow fast during the first five years. They need balanced diets to help them grow. Parents play a big role in shaping children's eating habits. It is the parent or care givers responsibility to offer nutritious foods at meals and snack time. How Can I Plan Healthy Menus? Use the USDA's ChooseMyplate.gov website to plan a healthy menu. You can get a customized plan for yourself or your child. Should My Child Eat Three Meals a Day? Your child should eat breakfast, lunch, dinner, and even snacks for a balanced diet. Children have lots of energy and need nutritious snacks to keep them going and growing. Breakfast is a "smart start" for your child every day. Breakfast can even boost their brain power to succeed in school. Studies show that children who eat breakfast have better concentration, work faster, make fewer mistakes, are more creative, and even score higher on tests. What Can I Do To Help My Child Eat New Foods? Children usually know how much food they need, but are not always good at choosing balanced meals. Children slowly learn to feed themselves and eat new foods, but you must take time to teach them. Here are some tips for introducing new foods: - Let children feed themselves using finger foods. - Soft foods are better since children under age 5 don't have all of their teeth. - Cut food into small pieces until your child is 4 years old. Give him or her foods that are easy to pick up, like small pieces of meat, cheese, or fruit. - When your child starts to use a spoon, place foods in plastic dishes with high sides. - As your child learns to chew, begin to serve foods with new textures. - A young child may not like hot or cold food. Serve foods at room temperature. - Make your child's meal fun to eat by serving naturally colorful foods. - If your child refuses something the first time, offer it again at a different meal. - Gradually introduce fiber by feeding your child a variety of fiber-rich fruits, vegetables, cereals, and other grain products. - Be sure to give your child enough fluids (especially water) to support a fiber rich diet. How Can I Prevent Choking? All young children gag and choke a little. That is one of the many reasons you need to stay with your child during mealtime. Choking often happens when children lie down with food in their mouths or run while they are eating, Be sure your child sits down to eat. Avoid hard foods like nuts, hard candy, tough meats, uncut raw carrots, raisins or popcorn. Cut round food like grapes the long way first, and then cut them into pieces. All food pieces should be smaller than a dime. Revised March 2012 Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Lily. She loved going to the market with her mommy. Have kids answer the following questions: 1) What is the problem? 2) What is my plan? 3) Am I following my plan? 4) How did I do? Have them apply these questions to academic and social problems. Give assignments that require evaluative skills. You might ask students to decide what grade they think they deserve on a paper or a report card, and to write a justification for that grade. Demonstrate "thinking aloud" during lessons. The more you talk about how you think your way through a problem or an assignment, the more likely your students are to use self-instruction as a learning strategy. Praise students when you see them using metacognitive skills. Say, "I like the way you went back and checked your work before handing it in. I saw you correct some spelling mistakes, and I saw you change a couple of sentences so they sounded better. That was good work." We how many mistakes have you made? A bag may have one or two handles or a shoulder strap. Bags come in different shapes and sizes depending on how they will be used. A small bag that can be carried with a single hand is sometimes called a handbag, purse, or pocketbook. The reason behind Heisenberg's uncertainty principle says that we can never know both the location and the momentum of a particle. Because light is an abundant particle, it is used for measuring other particles. The only way to measure it is to bounce the light wave off of the particle and record the results. If a high energy, or high frequency, light beam is used, we can tell precisely where it is, but cannot tell how fast it was going. This is because the high energy photon transfers energy to the particle and changes the particle's speed. If we use a low energy photon, we can tell how fast it is going, but not where it is. This is because we are using light with a longer wavelength. The longer wavelength means the particle could be anywhere along the stretch of the wave. In brewing beer, the amount of alcohol can be made more or less quite easily. The Belgian types of beer are made by adding more sugar. Through the fermentation, this will then turn to alcohol. Today, there are beers with between 2% and about 16% of alcohol (about the same alcohol content as wine). Spirits can have up to 80% alcohol. Some beer labels say there is no alcohol in them because it was taken out later. This is not completely true, though. Beers "without alcohol" usually do have less than 1% of alcohol. Access prior knowledge and build background about whales. Explore and apply the concept of whales. Students will: Demonstrate an understanding of the concept of whales. Orally use words that describe different types of whales and where they live. Extend oral vocabulary by speaking about terms that describe whales and whale body parts. Use key concept words [inlet, humpback, ocean, fins, underwater; submerge, ascend, Baleen, mammal]. Explain Use the slideshow to review the key concept words. Explain that students are going to learn about: Where whales live. Parts of a whale's body. Model After the host introduces the slideshow, point to the photo on screen. Ask students: What kind of animal do you see in this picture? (whale). What do you know about these animals? (answers will vary). Ask students: What are the dangers facing whales? (too much hunting, polluted environment). Say: In this activity, we're going to learn about whales. How can we protect whales? (not pollute the environment, join groups that are concerned with their safety). Guided Practice Guide students through the next two slides, showing them examples of whales and the way whales live. Always have the students describe how people are different from whales. Apply Play the games that follow. Have them discuss with their partner the different topics that appear during the Talk About It feature. After the first game, ask students to talk about what they think a whale's living environment is like. After the second game, have them discuss what they would like and dislike about having the body of a whale. Close Ask students: How do you move in the water? Summarize for students that since whales are mammals, they have to come above water to breathe. Encourage them to think about how they breathe underwater. Many birds are brown, green or grey. These colours make a bird harder to see: they camouflage the bird. Brown is the most common colour. Brown birds include: sparrows, emus, thrushes, larks, eagles and falcons and the female birds of many species such as: wrens, ducks, blackbirds and peafowls. When a brown bird is in long grass or among tree trunks or rocks, it is "camouflaged". Birds that live in long grass often have brown feathers streaked with black which looks like shadows. A bittern is almost invisible in long reeds because its camouflage is helped by its posture (beak and head pointed upwards). Other birds, including starlings and minahs, are quite dark in colour, but are flecked with little spots that look like raindrops on leaves. Bird may also camouflage their nests. He turned back to ground and ate his yummy yellow apple. Then he trotted away into the sunshine. Title: How to Find Out If Your Landlord is in Foreclosure Introduction: Foreclosures can be a stressful and confusing situation, especially when they involve your living arrangements. As a tenant, you have rights that must be respected during the foreclosure process. To protect yourself and make informed decisions, it's essential to know whether your landlord is facing foreclosure. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the steps required to find this information. Step 1: Check Official Notices Look for any official notices or letters left at your residence related to property tax bills, mortgage payments, or legal proceedings. These documents may provide clues about potential foreclosure actions against your landlord. Keep an eye out for terms like "Notice of Default," "Lis Pendens," or "Notice of Trustee Sale." * Key Tip: Familiarize yourself with common foreclosure-related terminology so you can quickly identify relevant documents. Step 2: Review Legal Publications In many jurisdictions, foreclosure sales are advertised in local newspapers or public court records. Regularly check these resources for listings that match your rental property address. Some states also require the posting of physical signs on the property; inspect your building for such indicators. * Guideline: Be aware that publication requirements vary by state and county. Consult your local government website or office for specific guidance on where and how to search for foreclosure notices. Step 3: Contact Government Agencies Reach out to government agencies responsible for overseeing housing and real estate transactions in your area. Explain your concerns and ask if there are any publicly available records indicating a pending foreclosure action against your landlord. Examples of appropriate entities include: * State Attorney General's Office * Local Housing Authority * County Recorder's Office * Mortgage Electronic Registration Systems (MERS) * Key Tip: Have your rental property address and landlord's name ready when contacting these organizations to streamline the process. Step 4: Speak With Neighbors and Community Members Engage in conversations with neighbors, fellow tenants, or nearby business owners who might have insights into your landlord's financial situation. They could provide valuable information regarding possible foreclosure filings or upcoming sale dates. * Guideline: Approach discussions with sensitivity and respect for privacy concerns, as foreclosure can be a delicate topic for those involved. Step 5: Consult a Real Estate Professional or Lawyer If you suspect a foreclosure but cannot confirm it using other methods, consider consulting a real estate professional or attorney familiar with local foreclosure laws. They can help interpret complex legal language and access private databases unavailable to the general public. * Key Tip: Before hiring a professional, verify their credentials, experience, and client reviews to ensure you receive accurate advice and reliable service. Step 6: Monitor Your Mailbox Keep an eye on mail addressed to your landlord, particularly certified letters or communications from lawyers, banks, or sheriff's offices. Such correspondence often contains crucial details about ongoing foreclosure proceedings. * Guideline: Remember that opening someone else's mail without permission is illegal; only review envelopes and return addresses for potential red flags. Conclusion: By following these steps, you increase the likelihood of discovering whether your landlord is indeed facing foreclosure. Armed with this knowledge, you can take proactive measures to safeguard your interests as a tenant, explore alternative living arrangements, and seek legal assistance if necessary. Stay vigilant, informed, and assertive throughout the process to minimize disruptions and maintain stability in your life. We don't have any ice cream with us today. Avoid the Danger Zones! School Bus Safety! To be safe when they travel to and from school, follow these simple safety rules: AT THE BUS STOP: - Always walk to the bus stop. Never run. - Walk on the sidewalk. If there is no sidewalk, walk on the left facing traffic. - Always go to the bus stop about five minutes before the bus is scheduled to arrive. - While at the bus stop, wait in a safe place away from the road. Do not run and play while waiting. - Never speak to strangers at the bus stop and never get into the car with a stranger. Always go straight home and tell you parents if a stranger tries to talk to you or pick you up. - Wait for the bus to arrive, watch for red flashing lights and the stop sign to be extended, and cross only when all traffic has stopped. Look left, right, and left again before crossing. ON THE BUS: - Go directly to a seat. Remain seated and facing forward for the entire ride. - Talk quietly (so the driver will not be distracted). - If you need to talk to the bus driver: wait for the bus to stop, raise your hand, and call the driver's name. - Never throw things on the bus or out the windows. Never play with the emergency exits. - Keep the aisles clear at all times. - If there is an emergency, listen to the driver and follow instructions. EXIT THE BUS: - When getting off the bus make sure you walk (not run) three more steps away from the door. This is the best place to be around a bus. Stay away from the bus wheels and watch out for moving cars! - Once you get off the bus, go straight home so an adult will know where you are. - Only get on and off the bus at your designated stop. If you need to get off the bus somewhere else, you will need to have a note from your parents. - If you leave something on the bus, never return to the bus to get it. The driver may not see you come back and they may begin moving the bus. Also, if you drop something near the bus, tell the bus driver before you attempt to pick it up, so they will know where you are. Always remain 10 steps away from the bus to be out of the "Danger Zone" and where the driver can see you. Always cross the street in front of the bus. Never go behind the bus. w why don't you read it first just to make sure. You can write you want some goldfish. Put it up by your ear, say hello. You know what it uh looks like? Title: How to Have the Best Time on an Overnight School Trip ========================================================= An overnight school trip is an exciting opportunity to learn, grow, and build strong relationships with your classmates. By following these steps, you can ensure that you have the best time possible while also being respectful, responsible, and safe. This guide will provide in-depth explanations for each step, as well as key tips and guidelines to help you make the most out of this experience. ### Step 1: Prepare Ahead of Time Preparation is essential for having a successful and enjoyable overnight school trip. The doctor in this field ensures the heart is healthy and functions properly. The heart is a vital organ whose role is to pump blood to the rest of the body. The purpose of blood is to deliver oxygen to the tissues. Without the heart functioning well, our tissues and organs would die and not function properly. Cardiologists treat heart attacks, sudden cardiac arrests, arrhythmias (rhythm issues related to a faulty electrical system of the heart), heart failure (where the heart fails to pump blood forward properly) and many other heart related illnesses. They specialize in life saving procedures like cardiac stents and cardiac ablation. There is a subspecialty within cardiology called "Interventional cardiology." These are cardiologists who specialize in interventions or procedures to save the function of the heart, such as cardiac stenting or angiography. Chi Chi I keep saying my name. The genus has extra lumbar (lower back) and thoracic (chest) vertebrae. This helps to explain the cat's spinal mobility and flexibility. Unlike human arms, cat forelimbs are attached to the shoulder by free-floating clavicle bones. These allow cats to pass their body through any space into which they can fit their heads. Dodgson was an Oxford don, a logician (mathematics expert), a writer, a poet, an Anglican clergyman, and a photographer. He is most famous for his story "Alice's Adventures in Wonderland" which he told to a young friend, Alice Liddell, when he took the girl and two sisters on a boat trip. Alice enjoyed the story and asked Dodgson to write it down. Carroll then wrote a second story about Alice called "Through the Looking-Glass". And so, hurried along whether she would or no, while her friend, Robert Fitz Godwine, accompanied her as far as he dared, the young Princess Edith was speedily brought into the presence of the king of England, William H., called, from the color of his hair and from his fiery temper, Rufus, or "the Red." For Edith and Mary were both princesses of Scotland, with a history, even before they had reached their teens, as romantic as it was exciting. Their mother, an exiled Saxon princess, had, after the conquest of Saxon England by the stern Duke William the Norman, found refuge in Scotland, and had there married King Malcolm Canmore, the son of that King Duncan whom Macbeth had slain. But when King Malcolm had fallen beneath the walls of Alnwick Castle, a victim to English treachery, and when his fierce brother Donald Bane, or Donald the Red, had usurped the throne of Scotland, then the good Queen Margaret died in the gray castle on the rock of Edinburgh, and the five orphaned children were only saved from the vengeance of their bad uncle Donald by the shrewd and daring device of the young Princess Edith, who bade their good uncle Edgar, the Atheling, guide them, under cover of the mist, straight through the Red Donald's knights and spearmen to England and safety. You would naturally suppose that the worst possible place for the fugitives to seek safety was in Norman England; for Edgar the Atheling, a Saxon prince, had twice been declared king of England by the Saxon enemies of the Norman conquerors, and the children of King Malcolm and Queen Margaret--half Scotch, half Saxon--were, by blood and birth, of the two races most hateful to the conquerors. If a person kisses or touches another person who is infected with the flu, a cold, measles, or a sore throat, he or she may get their disease. They may also give the person their disease if they cough on them. Other medical conditions such as AIDS, herpes, and hepatitis B, are only passed by closer contact. Having sex with an infected person, blood transfusions, or touching their blood or urine can sometimes pass on these conditions or diseases. Most fruits, vegetables, meat, and seafood are usually sold by weight (for example, apples cost 99 cents per pound), while other items are usually sold by quantity (for example, table salt costs $1.59 for each box). Not all items are the same price at each grocery store. Also, every week, some items at grocery stores cost less that what it normally would cost (we say that these items are "on sale"), so people would want to go buy them since they don't have to pay as much as they would normally have to get that item. Many grocery stores print a flyer that shows which items are on sale that week. These flyers are then sent to other people in the mail, who would read the flyer and go to the store to buy the items shown in it. The Eastern Orthodox Church, officially the Orthodox Church, is a Christian church. Their type of Christianity is also called Orthodox Christianity or Orthodoxy. Their members are called Orthodox Christians, although there is another group of Churches called Oriental Orthodox that is not in communion with the Orthodox Church. Go away you're eating up my whole garden. Rationale: Phoneme Awareness is one of the most important skills for children to master before they begin to learn to read. If they can master this skill then most will be incredibly successful readers and will more than likely find more fun and joy in their pursuit to read and write. In this lesson the children will be taught to identify the short o mouth movement, /o/. They will interact with the teacher in a number of games in which they will have the opportunity to recognize and single out the phoneme /o/ in spoken words. Materials: Tongue Twister on large piece of chart paper, primary writing paper, pencils, In the Big Top (Educational Insights, 1990), index cards with the letter o on them for students, crayons, students' journals, assessment sheets with pictures with hat, pot, stop, cup, mop, log, cot, and zip. 1. To begin this lesson the teacher will first explain that every mouth move that we make has a letter that goes along with it, or that stands for it. Just like how a baseball bat is used to play baseball, our letters are used to recognize which mouth movement to use when reading or speaking. Today we are going to learn how to recognize the /o/ mouth movement in many different words. It may be confusing at first, but with practice it will get much easier. Are you ready? 2. How many of you have ever been to the doctor? Has he ever asked you to open your mouth up really wide and say /o/, so that he could see your teeth or the back of your throat? Well, that is the mouth move that I am going to teach you to pick out in words today. Can everyone say /o/ for me? Now stretch it out and say it for a long time /o-o-o-o-o-o/. Very good! Now try saying hot, ho-o-o-o-o-t. Excellent! 3. Now everyone listen to me say this tongue twister one time and then we'll see if you can say it with me the second time. "Tom the octopus is on top of a rock at the bottom of the ocean." Now let's say it together but I want all of us to really stretch out our doctor mouth move /o/ when we say it. Here we go, "To-o-om the o-o-octopus is sitting o-o-o-n to-o-op of a ro-o-ock at the bo-o-otto-o-om of the ocean." Great job! 4. Now I would like everyone to try and write an o, the letter that represents our mouth movement /o/. First take out a sheet of (primary) paper and a pencil and get ready to write. As the teacher begins to write (so that all students can see her) she instructs them to start just underneath the fence and move your pencil up to the fence, then around and down to the sidewalk. Keep going in your circle around and up, back to where you began your o, just underneath the fence again. Now everyone hold up your o's and let me see. Super job everyone! Now I would like to see everyone write five more of those beautiful o's of your paper. You may begin. 5. Now we are going to play a matching game. I am going to give you a description of a word, and I would like one of you to raise your hand to tell me the word that matches my description. Ok? Let's begin. I am thinking of something that you cook in, on top of the stove. It begins with the /po/ sound. Pot. I am thinking of the opposite of cold. It begins with the sound /ho/. Hot. I am thinking of what a bunny rabbit and a kangaroo do. It begins with the sound /ho/. Hop. Excellent everyone! Now let's all make our doctor mouth movement that was in every one of those words, /o-o-o/. Great! Now let's play another game. I'm going to ask one group to decode my secret word and then to repeat our doctor mouth move again. Each team will get a turn and we'll see how all of you do. Team one, can you figure out this secret code word, /t/ /o/ /p/? Top, /o-o-o/. Good team one. Now team two, what about /g/ /o/ /t/? Got, /o-o-o/. Wonderful! 6. Next, read the story In the Big Top aloud to the students. Give each student an index card with the letter o on it and read the story again, this time asking the students to hold up their o card every time they hear the /o/ doctor sound. Every time you see my mouth make our /o/ doctor move hold up your o card to let me know that you have done so. Then allow the students to write a journal entry about the hot rod in the big top. Allow them to share their work. Encourage them to use invented spelling and to describe the characters Rob, Tod, Roz, Pop, Tom, and Mom getting into the hot rod. 7. Now for assessment. Give each individual child a sheet with pictures on it. I want all of you to look at these pictures. If your mouth makes the /o/ doctor move when you say the name of the picture then I want you to circle it. Let's do the first one together. What is that a picture of? A hat. Good. Should you circle it? Does your mouth make the /o/ movement? No. Excellent! What about the next one, a pot? And so on. Eldredge, J. Lloyd. Teaching decoding in Holistic Classrooms. Prentice- Hall, Inc. Englewood Cliffs, New Jersey. (1995) 63-67. Cushman, Sheila. In the Big Top. Educational Insights. Carson, CA. (1990). Click here to return to Inroads. Hello young mathematicians! Today, let's learn about something called "simplicial objects." Now, don't get intimidated by the fancy name - it's actually quite simple once we break it down together! Imagine you have a category, which is just a way of organizing things so that we can talk about how they relate to each other. For example, think about a collection of shapes like circles, squares, triangles, etc. We could create a category where each shape is an object, and there are arrows between them indicating whether one shape can fit inside another. So, there would be an arrow from the circle to the square, but not the other way around. Now, imagine taking this category and turning it upside down. Instead of having arrows point from a smaller shape to a larger shape, we now have arrows pointing from a larger shape to a smaller shape. This is what we mean when we say "contravariant functor." It's just a fancy way of saying "a rule that tells us how to flip our arrows." When we combine these two ideas - categories and contravariant functors - we get something called a "simplicial object." More specifically, a simplicial object is a rule that assigns an object in a category to every shape in our imaginary world of shapes. And remember, since we flipped our arrows earlier, this means that bigger shapes will correspond to objects in our category that come before smaller shapes. For example, let's consider the category of sets, which is just a collection of items without any particular structure. When we apply our simplicial object rule to this category, we get something called a "simplicial set." Simplicial sets are useful for studying topology, which is the branch of math dealing with properties of space that are preserved under continuous transformations. There's also such a thing as a "simplicial abelian group," which is similar to a simplicial set but deals instead with collections of numbers that follow certain rules. These turn out to be very important in algebraic topology, a field that uses tools from abstract algebra to study topological spaces. Finally, it's worth noting that simplicial objects form their own category, meaning that we can talk about maps or "morphisms" between different simplicial objects. Just like how functions take inputs and give outputs, morphisms tell us how to move from one simplicial object to another while preserving all the essential features. I hope this introduction to simplicial objects has piqued your curiosity! While it may seem abstract at first glance, understanding these concepts opens up entire worlds of mathematical possibilities. Keep exploring and never stop asking questions - after all, that's what being a mathematician is all about! Okay great and the next word is hat what is a hat? Did you need to talk to him about... In the center of southern Florida is a lake called Lake Okeechobee. It is the seventh largest freshwater lake in the United States and the second largest freshwater lake entirely within the lower 48 states. In 1896, Berlin sexologist Magnus Hirschfeld makes a system that measures the strength of sexual desire of an individual. This system measures on two different scales of 10 points. The scales are A (homosexual) and B (heterosexual). A heterosexual individual may be A0, B5. A homosexual individual may be A5, B0. An asexual is A0, B0. Someone with a strong attraction to both genders may be A9, B9. She becomes impatient and starts to cry.] There are different translations of this commandment; the Hebrew words are translated either as "thou shalt not kill" or "thou shalt not murder". Older Protestant translations of the Bible, those based on the Vulgate and Roman Catholic translations usually translate it "Thou shalt not kill". The Catholic Church believes that endangerment of human life or safety is a mortal sin that breaks The Fifth Commandment. Furthermore, the Catholic Church does not believe in a difference between murder and manslaughter the way the law does. With the exceptions of killing in self-defense (a form of manslaughter in many nations' laws) and killing in war, the Catholic Church believes all other forms of killing or attempting to kill violate The Fifth Commandment. Unsafe driving could also lead to unintentional killing. Jewish and newer Protestant versions tend to use "You shall not murder". There are different opinions as to which translation is more faithful to the original. Covert 1 You may scratch your head and ask, "How can a list be a poem?" It sounds crazy, doesn't it? However, list poems can be fun to write. You don't have to rhyme. You don't have to count beats. You only need a topic. Then, you write a list about that topic. You may use single words or phrases. You may add items to the list that are silly or strange. The Lion & The Rabbit A Fable from India The animals of the forest made a bargain with a ferocious lion who killed for pleasure. It was agreed that one animal each day would willingly come to the ferocious lion's den to be his supper and, in turn, the lion would never hunt again. The first to go to the lion's den was a timid rabbit, who went slowly. "Why are you late?" the lion roared when the rabbit arrived. "I'm late because of the other lion," said the rabbit. "In my jungle? Take me to this other lion." The rabbit led the lion to a deep well and told him to look in. The lion saw his own reflection in the water and roared! The sound of his roar bounced right back at him as an echo. "I alone am king of this jungle," he roared again. His echo answered him, "I alone am king of this jungle." With that, the lion became so enraged, he charged into the deep well with a great splash! The lion attacked his own reflection and was never heard from again. [first use of "only you can do it"?] Title: How to Provide Care for New Mothers: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: The postpartum period is a critical time for new mothers as they recover from childbirth and adjust to caring for a newborn. As a caregiver, friend, or family member, providing support during this transition can significantly improve the physical and emotional well-being of both the mother and her baby. This guide will provide you with detailed steps and essential information about how to offer compassionate, effective care for mums. Step 1: Understand the Physical Recovery Process After giving birth, a woman's body undergoes significant changes that require rest and healing. Key areas to focus on include: * Vaginal recovery (tearing, episiotomy) * Uterine contractions and afterpains * Perineal pain and discomfort * Breast engorgement and soreness * Fatigue and exhaustion Providing care for these issues involves offering comfort measures, encouraging self-care, and assisting with daily tasks. By understanding what she is experiencing physically, you can better empathize and help meet her needs. Step 2: Offer Emotional Support New mothers often experience mood swings, anxiety, and feelings of overwhelm due to hormonal shifts and sleep deprivation. Here are some ways to offer emotional support: * Be an active listener * Validate her emotions * Encourage open communication * Help manage expectations * Remind her that asking for help is okay Step 3: Assist with Household Tasks Helping with chores like cooking, cleaning, laundry, and grocery shopping reduces stress and frees up energy for bonding with the baby. Consider setting up meal trains, delegating specific tasks, or hiring professional cleaners temporarily. Step 4: Support Breastfeeding Efforts Breastfeeding can be challenging for many women. To encourage successful breastfeeding, consider the following strategies: * Educate yourself about proper latch techniques and positioning * Encourage skin-to-skin contact between mum and baby * Offer snacks and hydrating drinks while feeding * Connect her with lactation consultants or support groups if needed Step 5: Promote Rest and Sleep Fatigue is common among new parents. You can help by: * Encouraging naps when possible * Taking turns tending to nighttime feedings * Preparing healthy meals that promote good sleep quality * Minimizing distractions and noise during quiet times Step 6: Foster Bonding Opportunities Facilitate opportunities for mum and baby to connect through activities such as kangaroo care, bathing together, reading stories aloud, singing songs, and gentle massages. These experiences foster attachment and contribute positively to early childhood development. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Respect her boundaries and personal space * Avoid unsolicited advice; instead, ask how you can best support her * Prioritize safety above efficiency when handling her or the baby * Keep conversations light and positive; avoid trigger topics * Check in regularly to assess changing needs and preferences * Remember that every mother's journey is unique - adapt your approach accordingly Conclusion: By following this comprehensive guide, you will be well-equipped to provide compassionate, informed care for new mothers during their postpartum recovery. Your efforts can make a substantial difference in promoting her overall health, happiness, and ability to navigate the challenges of parenthood. Title: How to Blow Perfect Bubbles Introduction: Blowing bubbles is an enjoyable activity that can bring delight to both children and adults alike. It's simple, entertaining, and requires only a few materials. This tutorial will guide you through the process of blowing perfect bubbles every time, with clear instructions and helpful tips along the way. Materials Needed: 1. A bubble solution (homemade or store-bought) 2. A bubble wand 3. Clean water 4. Optional: food coloring or glycerin 5. Patience and practice! Step 1: Prepare Your Solution Start by preparing your bubble solution. You may choose to buy one from a store, but making your own at home can be more fun and cost-effective. To make homemade bubble solution, mix 1 cup of dish soap (preferably without lotion), 6 cups of warm water, and 1 tablespoon of light corn syrup or glycerin if available. Stir gently until all ingredients are well combined. Allow the mixture to rest overnight for best results. Key Tip: Using high-quality dish soap and adding glycerin or corn syrup will create stronger and longer-lasting bubbles due to their thicker consistency. Avoid using cold water as it can cause the soap to become less effective. Step 2: Customize Your Solution (Optional) If you want to add some creativity to your bubbling experience, consider customizing your solution. Add a drop or two of food coloring to give your bubbles vibrant colors. Be careful not to add too much; otherwise, the dye might stain surfaces when popped. For even shinier bubbles, try incorporating a touch of honey into your solution. Step 3: Dip The Wand Dip your bubble wand fully into the prepared solution, ensuring that the wands are thoroughly coated with the liquid. Make sure there aren't any drips hanging off the end, which could weaken the structure of the bubbles. Key Guideline: Keep your dip shallow enough so the excess solution drains back into the container instead of pooling around the wand's base. Too much solution will result in heavier bubbles prone to popping prematurely. Step 4: Find The Right Angle Hold the wand horizontally about an inch away from your face. Tilt it slightly upwards towards the sky, creating a small angle between the wand and the ground. This position allows gravity to help maintain the shape of the bubbles while preventing them from being blown directly onto your face. Step 5: Pucker Up And Blow Purse your lips together tightly, forming a small 'O' shape with your mouth. Gently blow air outward through your pursed lips, directing the stream of air toward the wand but not straight into it. Maintain a steady pace and force - neither too strong nor too weak - just enough to detach the formed bubbles from the wand. Key Tip: Remember to breathe in through your nose during this process to avoid swallowing the bubble solution accidentally. Step 6: Practice Patiently At first, you might struggle with achieving perfectly round bubbles or keeping them afloat for extended periods. Don't get discouraged! With patience and consistent practice, you'll soon master the art of blowing beautiful bubbles. Conclusion: Now you know how to blow perfect bubbles like a pro! By following these steps and practicing regularly, you're bound to impress yourself and others with your newfound skill. Happy bubbling! And you can feel it pull it down. is designed to help children learn to read and spell words. They will learn to recognize i=/i/ in written words. They will learn a meaningful representation and then practice spelling and reading words with i=/i/ using a letterbox lesson. Materials: Chart with Icky Sticky Inchworm ; Tin Man Fix It (Educational Insights); list of words to use in letterbox lesson (3-phoneme six, lip, him, pin; 4-phoneme fist, slim, grin, sing) list of words to use assessment (3-phoneme fin, rim, did, pin; 4-phoneme tips, twin, list, trim); Letterboxes, letters laminated and cut out to use in letterbox lesson. 1. First, introduce the correspondence i=/i/ by saying the meaningful representation, Icky Sticky Inchworm . Show the student how to say the i in each word like I-I-I-cky-Sti-cky I-I-Ichworm . Have the student repeat this with you. We are going to learn how to spell and read words with /i/ in them. Review with the child how to spell six , then help the child read the word. 2. Next, the letters that you need to spell the words in the letterbox lesson out for the child to choose from. Lay 3 letterboxes attached together down so the child can spell the three phoneme words you give him/her. After completing the three phoneme words use the same steps to do the four phoneme words. 3. Now, put the letterboxes away and spell the words for the child asking him/her to read the words from the letterbox lesson to you. 4. Give the child the book, Tin Man Fix-it and ask him/her to read it to you. It is important that you allow the child to hold the book while they are reading. As they read, take a running record of the word the child misses so that so that you will know which correspondences to teach next time. 5. For assessment, have the child spell different words from the letterbox lesson. After the child has completed both the three and four phoneme words spell the words out on the table and ask the child to read them. It is important that you don t ask the child to read what he/she spells because you would ve just spoken the word so they know how to read it. This assessment will allow you to see if the child has understood the concept of i=/i/. ~Eldridge, J. Lloyd, Teaching Decoding in Holistic Classrooms. History is the study of past events. People know what happened in the past by looking at things "from" the past including sources (like books, newspapers, and letters) and artifacts (like pottery, tools, and human or animal remains.) Libraries, archives, and museums collect and keep these things for people to study history. A person who studies history is called a historian. A person who studies pre-history and history through things left behind by ancient cultures is called an archaeologist. A person who studies mankind and society is called an anthropologist. The study of the sources and methods "used to study and write" history is called historiography. Look at Mommy and look through it. Title: How to Download Play Store Apps using a Computer Introduction: Google's Play Store is a digital distribution service operated and developed by Google. It serves as the official app store for certified devices running on the Android operating system and its variants. While you can directly download apps from the Play Store on your mobile device, there are instances when using a computer may be more convenient or necessary. This tutorial will guide you through the process of downloading Play Store apps using a computer with clear, step-by-step instructions. Key Tips and Guidelines: 1. Make sure your computer has an internet connection. 2. You need to have a Google account linked to your Android device. 3. The method explained here uses the web version of the Google Play Store. 4. Not all apps are available for download via the web player; some require manual installation (APK files) which isn't covered in this tutorial due to security concerns. 5. Downloaded apps will automatically install onto your device if it's connected to WiFi and USB debugging is enabled. Otherwise, you'll need to manually transfer the APK file to your phone. Steps to Download Play Store Apps using a Computer: Step 1: Open Your Web Browser On your computer, open any web browser like Google Chrome, Mozilla Firefox, Microsoft Edge, etc. For optimal experience and compatibility, we recommend using the latest version of Google Chrome. Step 2: Navigate to Google Play Website In the address bar at the top of your browser window, type in "play.google.com" without quotes, then press Enter. This will take you to the homepage of the Google Play website. Step 3: Sign In To Your Google Account If you aren't already signed into your Google account, look towards the upper right corner of the page where it says 'Sign In'. Click on that link and enter your credentials. Remember, this should be the same account associated with your Android device. Step 4: Search For Desired App Use the search box located near the center of the page to find the app you want to download. Type in the name of the application exactly as it appears on your phone, ensuring correct spelling and capitalization. After typing, hit Enter or click the magnifying glass icon next to the input field. Step 5: Select Correct App Version From the search results, select the appropriate app. Be cautious about selecting sponsored links or similar sounding names. Check the developer name, number of downloads, ratings, and reviews before proceeding further. Step 6: Initiate Installation Process Once you've chosen the correct app, click the green button labeled 'Install'. A popup dialog box will appear asking for permission. Here, choose the specific device you wish to send the app to. If you only have one device registered under your account, it should show up automatically. Step 7: Authorize Installation Remotely A notification prompt will now appear on your Android device seeking approval for the download and installation. Grant access by tapping 'Accept' or 'OK'. Wait until the download completes. Automatic installation begins once done. Conclusion: By following these steps meticulously, you can successfully download apps from the Play Store straight to your Android device using your computer. However, remember not every app supports remote installation so don't panic if everything doesn't work perfectly every time. Happy downloading! Dogs whose parents were the same breed will also be that breed: these dogs are called purebred or pure pedigree dogs. Dogs with parents from different breeds no longer belong to one breed: they are called "mutts", "mixed-breed dogs", "hybrids", or "mongrels". Some of the most popular breeds are sheepdogs, collies, poodles and retrievers. It is becoming popular to breed together two different breeds of dogs and call the new dog's breed a name that is a mixture of the parents' breeds' two names. A puppy with a poodle and a pomeranian as parents might be called a Pomapoo. These kinds of dogs, instead of being called mutts, are known as designer dog breeds. These dogs are normally used for prize shows and designer shows. Do you think that's what your teachers do when they go home from a hard day? YAY for Expression! Rationale: Fluency is being able to read and reread decodable words in connected text. To be fluent you have to be able to read accurate and automatic. To be a successful reader which means fluent, there are five components: reading faster, reading with expression, reading smoothly, reading silently and being able to read voluntarily. This lesson is going to focus on being able to read with expression. Materials: Blackboard, chalk, sentences written by the teacher, Five Minutes' Peace, 1 copy of Lee and the Team for each student, Assessment checklist for each student Sample Sentences: Do you like to go to the playground? Stop kicking me! Can you pass me that book please? I can't wait to go to the beach! Leave me alone! Do you want to go and play with me? 1. The teacher will begin by sharing about expressive reading with the students. To let people we are reading too know what is happening in a story we have to change our voice. When we change our voice we either make it lower or higher to show our emotion. I will also share why we read with expression. First, I will tell the students to listen to two sentences. When the children think they hear the one with expression, they will raise their hand. I will then ask them if they think they would like listening to a story if it was read like the sentence without expression? The class will then realize that expression makes a story interesting and enjoyable to listen too. 2. Now I will read Five Minutes' Peace. I will tell the children that if you hear me using good expression and emotion I want you to hold both thumbs up, if think I am reading with no expression and very plain then hold both down. The teacher will read a few sentences without expression going to the beach.") then change and read with great expression ("I am to the beach!"). This will show the students what a big makes when someone reads with expression. 3. Now I will write several sentences on the board. I will ask for a student to read that sentence without expression then reread it with the expression that they think is needed to make the sentence make sense. We will do the first one together. Ex. I love the beach! The teacher will read the first time with no emotion or expression then reread with great expression and exclamation. 4. Next the teacher will review with the class about punctuation marks and the feelings associated with them. The teacher will explain how a person might change their voice in different ways to read a sentence with a question mark ("Will you please hand be that book?") or an exclamation point("Hand me that book!"). There will be a class discussion and several examples given by both the students and the teacher. 5. Now we will be reading a book called Lee and the Team. Book Talk: "This book is about a boy Lee. He is on a baseball team. On the day of their game, they are late. The team is supposed to run but they do not want to. What will happen since they do not want to run? Lets find out!" 6. The teacher will ask the students to pull out their copies of Lee and the Team. First the students will read silently. I will then call on students to read out loud to practice using expression by having to look out for the punctuation. As the students are reading out loud I will have my checklist checking off how they read. If they are not reading with enough expression or not reading it correctly I will stop them, ask them why they think I stopped them, help them fix it, and they will then reread that sentence. http://www.auburn.edu/rdggenie/innov/parrishgf.html Use Expression! By 'imageless dances' he meant dances which have no set form, but aim at getting the dancers into a state of ecstasy. In this state the dancer(s) seem changed, in a trance, and are often thought of (by their society) as being 'possessed by spirits'. These dances are done on certain occasions: marriage, war, famine, illness or death, and so on. They are found in all early ('primitive') societies. Do you think the baby wants to drink? One way to customize your WMP experience is by adding skins from TheSkinsFactory.com. This tutorial will guide you through the process of downloading and applying these third-party skins to enhance your multimedia enjoyment. ### Part 1: Choosing Your Desired Skin 1. **Visit TheSkinsFactory.com**: Open your web browser and navigate to <https://theskinfactory.com/>. This website offers hundreds of free skins created by designers worldwide specifically for Windows Media Player. 2. **Browse Available Skins**: On the homepage, browse different categories such as 'Newest', 'Most Downloaded', or 'Top Rated' skins based on your preference. You can also filter them according to version compatibility with your installed WMP. Click on any skin thumbnail for more details about its features and screenshots. 3. **Read Description & Check Compatibility**: Before downloading, ensure the chosen skin supports your WMP version (either WMP 10, 11, or 12). Also, read the description thoroughly; some creators may include installation instructions or known issues there. 4. **Download Selected Skin**: Once satisfied with your choice, click the 'Download Now' button usually located near the bottom of the page. We do not condone or endorse any harmful behavior towards parents or guardians. Always maintain respect and understanding when interacting with your parents. According to the special theory of relativity, it is impossible to say whether two distinct events occur at the same time if those events are separated in space. To speak of "the shape of the universe (at a point in time)" is naive from the point of view of special relativity. Due to the relativity of simultaneity we cannot speak of different points in space as being "at the same point in time" nor, therefore, of "the shape of the universe at a point in time". Jazz is a type of music that was invented around 1900 in New Orleans in the south of the USA. There were many black musicians living there who played a style of music called blues music. Blues music was influenced by African music (because the black people in the United States had come to the United States as slaves. They were taken from Africa by force). Blues music was a music that was played by singing, using the harmonica, or the acoustic guitar. Many blues songs had sad lyrics about sad emotions (feelings) or sad experiences, such as losing a job, a family member dying, or having to go to jail (prison). He was the United States Senator from Kansas between 1969 to 1996 as a member of the Republican Party. He was the Republican Leader of the United States Senate during the final eleven years of his Senate career from 1985 until 1996. Before his senate career, he was a member of the United States House of Representatives from 1961 to 1969. Go Out in Public with a Stye A stye is a common eye condition that can cause discomfort and make you feel self-conscious about your appearance. However, sometimes going out in public is unavoidable even when dealing with this uncomfortable issue. This guide will provide you with step-by-step instructions on how to go out in public with a stye while minimizing its impact on your day-to-day activities. **Step 1: Understand what a stye is** Before addressing how to manage having a stye in public, let's first understand what it is. A stye (also called a hordeolum) is an infection or inflammation of one or more oil glands at the base of an eyelash or within the eyelid itself. It typically appears as a small red bump or pimple near the edge of the eyelid. **Step 2: Recognize symptoms** Recognizing the signs and symptoms of a stye can help distinguish it from other conditions like pink eye or chalazion. Symptoms include: - Redness and swelling on the eyelid - Pain or tenderness around the affected area - Crusting along the lashes - Watery eyes - Sensitivity to light - Feeling like there's something in your eye **Step 3: Consult a healthcare professional** While most styes resolve independently without medical intervention, consulting a healthcare provider can offer reassurance and ensure proper diagnosis. They may prescribe antibiotics if they suspect bacterial involvement. **Step 4: Apply warm compresses** Applying warm compresses several times daily can speed up healing by increasing blood flow and reducing swelling. Soak a clean washcloth in hot water, wring it out, and hold it against the closed eye for five minutes. Repeat this process multiple times throughout the day. **Step 5: Practice good hygiene** Prevent spreading the bacteria causing the stye by washing hands frequently and avoiding touching the infected eye. Additionally, avoid sharing makeup or towels during this time. **Step 6: Choose appropriate makeup and tools** Opt for hypoallergenic cosmetics and sanitize applicators regularly. Avoid using old mascara or eyeliners which could harbor harmful bacteria. If possible, refrain from wearing eye makeup until the stye heals completely. **Step 7: Dress appropriately** Wear sunglasses to protect your sensitive eye from harsh sunlight, wind, or dust. Consider dressing casually yet presentably so you don't draw unnecessary attention to yourself. **Step 8: Plan ahead** If feasible, schedule important meetings or events after the expected duration of the stye. This way, you minimize the chances of others seeing your stye during critical moments. **Step 9: Manage pain effectively** Over-the-counter pain relievers such as ibuprofen or acetaminophen can alleviate any discomfort associated with a stye. Follow package directions carefully regarding dosages. **Step 10: Be mindful of social interactions** Try not to rub or scratch the affected eye, especially in front of others. Explaining might be necessary but try to maintain composure and confidence. People are generally understanding and less likely to notice or remember than we think. By following these steps, you should feel equipped to handle going out in public with a stye confidently. Remember, patience and self-care are crucial elements of managing this temporary condition. They do not even exist elsewhere in Africa. They only exist in Madagascar. In fact, most of the mammals living in Madagascar do not live anywhere else in the world. The wolf is the largest member of the Canidae family. The coyote evolved from the wolf over 500,000 years ago. The Gray Wolf and the Red Wolf are the two types of wolves living in North America. 2. Wolves are social animals that live in packs. Packs can have as little as 2 members or as many as 30 members. The average pack size is 6 to 8. Pack Territories vary with location. In the Alaskan or Canadian Wilderness the territory for one pack ranges from 300 to 1,000 square miles while in the continental U.S. the territory is between 25 and 150 square miles. 3. The North American Wolf can weigh between 40 to 175 pounds. Their length varies between 4'6" and 6'6" from muzzle to tip of tail. The height of an average Wolf is between 26 and 32 inches at the shoulder. 4. Wolves have large feet, the average being 4 inches wide by 5 inches long. Wolves have 42 teeth and rounded ears. The wolf has extremely powerful jaws. Their fur color varies from pure white or pure black to tan or gray. The wolf has 2 types of hair, "Guards and "Undercoat". The hair of the wolf is shed in the spring and summer and sheds out in sheets unlike most dogs. The wolf's sense of smell is more than 100 times greater than a human. 5. In the wild, wolves live on the average of 6 to 8 years. In captivity, wolves can live much longer. One wolf lived to be 16 years old. 6. Wolves breed once a year and they have an average litter size is 4 to 6 cubs. The cubs weigh approximately one pound at birth and cannot see or hear. Mortality rates for wolf pups can be as high as 50%. All members of a wolf pack take part in caring for the young. 7. The wolf primarily travels at a 5 mile per hour trot. A wolf can go as fast as 35 miles per hour for a short distance. 8. Wolves are carnivores (meat-eater) that eat beaver, bison, elk, caribou, moose and deer. They also eat small rodents. The wolf does little chewing. They can eat twenty pounds of prey at a feeding. The wolf is an ultimate predator at the top of the food chain and is only threatened by people. 9. Wolves can communicate in many ways. They leave scent marks, make a variety of sounds, facial and body postures and rituals. Wolves communicate with each other more by making sounds and nuzzling. 10. Loss of habitat and being hunted by people are leading factors in the Wolves being on the "Endangered Species List". Now you height put all the letters back. Mar. 12. Title: Browse the Internet Using Windows Help Introduction: In this extensive guide, we will walk you through the process of using Windows Help to browse the internet. This is especially useful if you are new to computing or prefer using built-in tools for online research. We'll provide clear steps, detailed explanations, helpful tips, and crucial guidelines to ensure your experience is smooth and productive. Step 1: Opening Windows Help Click on the Start button (Windows icon) usually located at the bottom left corner of your screen. In the search bar, type "Help" and click on the "Windows Help" application that appears in the search results. This action opens the Windows Help window, providing access to various topics and resources. Key Tip: Make sure your computer has an active internet connection before proceeding, as some help content may be downloaded from Microsoft servers. Step 2: Accessing the Web Content Once the Windows Help window is open, look for the navigation pane typically found on the left side of the window. Here, you might find several categories such as 'Get started', 'Learn more', or similar options depending upon the version of Windows you have installed. Click on any category relevant to your interests or needs; doing so displays related subtopics within the main panel. Guideline: Be patient while navigating through different sections since loading times can vary based on your network speed. Step 3: Performing a Search If you already know what information you seek, utilize the search function instead. Locate the search box, often positioned near the top right corner of the Windows Help window. Type in keywords pertaining to your query, then press Enter or click on the magnifying glass icon next to the text field. The resultant list shows articles matching your search criteria. Helpful Hint: Use specific and precise terms for better search accuracy. For instance, instead of searching for "computer problems," try something like "slow startup Windows 10." Step 4: Reading Articles After locating a potentially helpful article, click on its title to view its contents in detail. Most articles contain headings, bullet points, images, and links to other associated topics. Read thoroughly until you understand the material or locate the answer(s) you need. Important Note: Some articles may include external hyperlinks leading to official Microsoft websites or third-party sources. Exercise caution when following these URLs, ensuring they originate from trusted entities. Step 5: Following Hyperlinks When an article contains hyperlinks (usually underlined blue text), hover over them with your mouse cursor to preview the linked webpage's address without leaving the current page. To visit the linked page, simply click on the hyperlink. Remember, always verify the credibility of external sites before sharing personal data or making financial transactions. Additional Advice: If you encounter broken links or outdated information, consider reporting it directly to Microsoft via their support channels. Your contributions could improve future iterations of Windows Help. Step 6: Returning to Previous Pages To go back to a previous article or section, either click on the Back button (often represented by a left-facing arrow) in your browser's toolbar or press Alt + Left Arrow keys simultaneously on your keyboard. These actions allow you to retrace your browsing history within the Windows Help environment. Conclusion: By following this comprehensive tutorial, you should now feel confident utilizing Windows Help to navigate the internet effectively. As a recap, remember to maintain patience during load times, employ specific search phrases, critically assess external links, and leverage navigation features wisely. Happy browsing! Individual freedom, voluntary association, and being against the state are important beliefs of anarchism. There are also big differences between anarchist philosophies on things like whether violence can be used to bring about anarchy; the best type of economy; the relationship between technology and hierarchy; the idea of equality; and the usefulness of some organization. The word "authority" is not clear, but anarchists are not against some types of authority (e.g. the authority of someone skilled in self-defence over someone that wants to learn self-defence), they are only against control by force. The Olympics have not brought lasting peace to the world, even during the Games. Three Olympic Games were not held due to war. The 1916 Games were cancelled because of World War I, and the summer and winter games of 1940 and 1944 were cancelled because of World War II. Terrorism has also threatened the Olympic Games. In 1972, when the Summer Games were held in Munich, West Germany, eleven members of the Israeli Olympic team were taken hostage by the terrorist group Black September. This event is now known as the Munich massacre. The terrorists killed two of the athletes soon after they had taken them hostage and killed the other nine during a failed rescue attempt. A German police officer and 5 terrorists also died.
